1:
A rare title opens the verse, and the grammatical-historical Robertson lingers over it: the best manuscripts read the Lord knew — a designation John's narrative gives Jesus in only five places (John 6:23; John 21:12), and he dismisses as hypercriticism Bernard's theory that these are later glosses. What the Lord knew is reported in the very words carried to the Pharisees — Ellicott and Clarke both observe that the clause "Jesus maketh and baptizeth more disciples than John" is quoted as the informers framed it. How he knew it divides the cloud gently: Wesley answers in six words, "though none informed him of it"; the Reformed Gill agrees, hearing the omniscience of one who knew every man and the secret motions of the heart; Barnes alone leaves it open whether the knowledge was divine or came by report. The Puritan Matthew Henry adds a shrewd caution: the report probably exceeded the truth, for it is not likely Jesus had yet actually baptized more than John — but so the thing was represented, to make him formidable (2 Kings 6:12); and none can dig so deep as to hide their counsels from the Lord (Isaiah 29:15).

Behind the report the witnesses see menace. Robertson reconstructs the moment: the Pharisees, who had already turned violently on the Baptist and likely maneuvered him into Herod's prison (Luke 3:19), now turn to Jesus "with envy and hate." Henry draws the standing lesson — the success of the gospel exasperates its enemies, and it is a good sign it is getting ground when the powers of darkness are enraged; he reckons Jesus had spent some six months building on John's foundation, making disciples before baptizing them, and quotes Tertullian: the Christian is made, not born.

Why, then, withdraw? The Latin Augustine answers that had the Pharisees' knowledge made them followers, Christ would have stayed for their sake; since it made them persecutors, he departed — teaching believers to come that it is no sin for a servant of God to flee the fury of persecution, an example given not from fear but for our instruction. The Antiochene Chrysostom adds two further ends: to pacify human envy, and to guard the dispensation of the incarnation itself — had Jesus been seized and escaped, the reality of his flesh might have been doubted. Calvin turns the retreat into a rule of vocation: Christ would not expose himself before the proper time, and his moderation instructs us neither to be deterred by fear from duty nor to throw ourselves rashly into danger — we must not advance farther than our calling demands. He also explains why only the Pharisees are named: not that the other scribes were friendly, but this sect was then in the ascendant, filled with rage under the pretense of godly zeal, and the crowds that flocked to Christ as John resigned his office to him exasperated them beyond what John's own success had done.
2:
"Is there a mistake made, and then corrected?" The Latin Augustine raises the difficulty himself — the Evangelist has just said Jesus baptized more than John, and now says Jesus himself baptized not — and resolves it with a paradox: both are true, for "He baptized, in that He cleansed; He baptized not, in that He dipped not." The disciples supplied the ministry of the body, Christ the aid of the Majesty of which it was said, the same is he which baptizeth. Ellicott reads the parenthesis more historically: it corrects not the writer's statement but the report carried to the Pharisees, which had spoken naturally enough of "his" baptism, since the crowds were drawn by him though the rite was administered ministerially by his disciples (Acts 10:48; 1 Corinthians 1:15-17). The Antiochene Chrysostom goes further — the reporters deliberately framed the fact to raise envy — and supplies the deeper reason Jesus abstained: John had declared, he shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost and with fire, and the Spirit was not yet given; the disciples' water-baptism meanwhile had no more virtue than John's, both aiming only to bring men to Christ.

The moderns weigh the practical wisdom of the abstention. Barnes suggests that had Jesus baptized, those he touched might have thought themselves the more honoured, breeding division among his followers. The Puritan Matthew Henry multiplies the reasons to six: to distinguish his baptism from John's, who baptized as a servant while Christ acted as master; to give himself to preaching, the more excellent work (1 Corinthians 1:17); to honour and train his disciples; to keep converts from glorying in the baptizer, the very evil Paul feared (1 Corinthians 1:13-14); to reserve himself for baptizing with the Holy Ghost (Acts 1:5); and to teach that the efficacy of sacraments depends not on the hand that administers them. The grammatical-historical Robertson notices that the imperfect tense makes non-baptizing Jesus' settled habit, that this is the New Testament's only instance of the emphatic conjunction rendered "although indeed," and that baptism under Jesus' direction is not mentioned again until the Great Commission (Matthew 28:19) — possibly, he ventures, Jesus stopped the rite because of the excitement over his Messianic claims.

Calvin distils the doctrine the church has drawn from this verse: baptism is not to be estimated by the person of the minister, for its whole power depends on its Author, in whose name it is conferred. Our baptism washes and renews us no less than if it had been given by the hand of the Son of God himself — the symbol received from a mortal man is to be viewed as if Christ displayed his hand from heaven and stretched it out to us — and this suffices to refute the Anabaptists, who say a wicked minister vitiates the sacrament, just as Augustine employed the same argument against the Donatists. Alcuin, meanwhile, asks whether the Spirit attended the disciples' baptism at all, and answers that before the visible fiery tongues the Spirit could be possessed secretly; and Augustine adds that the disciples themselves must already have been baptized — most probably by Christ, for he who stooped to wash his disciples' feet would not have failed to baptize his servants.
3:
One Greek verb carries the weight of this short verse. Robertson, for the grammatical-historical school, notes the unusual use of a word that ordinarily means to send away, dismiss, forsake, forgive: Jesus left Judea — almost abandoned it — and from now on he mainly avoids Jerusalem and Judea until the end, each later appearance there issuing in open breach with the Pharisees (John 5:1-47; John 10:22-42). Ellicott defends the little word again, dropped by some manuscripts precisely because it puzzled copyists: the earlier return to Galilee is that of John 1:43, and since this Judean ministry preceded the Baptist's imprisonment (John 3:24), this second withdrawal is the very starting-point of the Galilean ministry the earlier Gospels record (Matthew 4:12; Mark 1:14). Robertson agrees on the harmony: the Synoptics tell nothing of this early Judean work; John supplements their records purposely.

On the motive there is no real division, only degrees of emphasis. Wesley is briefest — he left "to shun the effects of their resentment." The Reformed Gill computes that Jesus had spent about eight months in Judea since the passover, and insists the departure was not through fear of the Pharisees but to avoid irritating and provoking them into measures against his life, "whereas his time was not yet come, and he had other work to do elsewhere." Barnes concurs: envy and malice were growing fast enough to endanger him; since his hour to die had not arrived he retired to a country farther from Jerusalem and much less under Pharisaic control, for though he feared not death, he never needlessly threw himself into danger — and he could do as much good in Galilee. The Puritan Matthew Henry works the same reasoning into counsel: Christ's hour was not yet come (John 7:30), his young Judean disciples were not able to bear hardships, and by quitting the country he gave example to his own rule, when they persecute you in one city, flee to another — we may not change our religion for our preservation, but we may change our place. Henry seals it with Chemnitius: the pious have those in this life from whom they can fly, but none to fly to for refuge except God.

The Antiochene Chrysostom sees a foreshadowing in the route itself: as the apostles, when expelled by the Jews, went to the Gentiles, so Christ, withdrawing from Judea, goes to the Samaritans — yet, to leave the Jews no excuse, he does not go to stay, but only takes Samaria on his road. And the Latin Alcuin, characteristically, hears a mystery under the itinerary: our Lord left Judea, that is, the unbelief of those who condemned him, and went into Galilee, that is, the fickleness of the world, thereby teaching his disciples to pass from vices to virtues. Between the textual critic counting manuscripts and the monk allegorizing the map, the whole cloud agrees on the substance: this is a strategic, unhurried retreat — prudence in the service of a fixed hour, not flight from it.
4:
Does must needs mean a map or a mission? That is the question the witnesses turn over. The grammatical-historical voices answer: a map. Barnes states it plainly — Samaria lay between Judea and Galilee, and the direct and usual way passed through it, though Jews sometimes took the circuitous route east of the Jordan. Clarke will allow "this plain reason, and no other": it was the only proper road, a three days' journey by Josephus's reckoning. Wesley compresses the case to a phrase: the road lay directly through it. Lightfoot cites the same historian for the custom of the Galileans passing through Samaria to the feasts, and garnishes the point with pages from his countryman Biddulph's journey of 1601 over those same pleasant hills. Robertson adds the nuance of direction: going north one naturally went through; coming south, travellers usually crossed into Perea to avoid Samaritan hostility toward pilgrims, a bitterness Jesus himself once met (Luke 9:51-56).

Yet several witnesses cannot stop at geography. The Reformed Gill grants the necessity of the road, but finds another necessity lying upon Christ: the calling and conversion of a certain woman and other Samaritans "whom the Father had given to him, and he was to redeem by his blood," the time of whose effectual calling was now come. Ellicott, though he begins from the shortest road (Luke 9:52), likewise ends in purpose: it lay within the design of Christ's life to teach in Samaria the principles of true religion and worship, which would cut away the foundations of all local jealousies and establish for all nations the spiritual service of the universal Father (John 4:21-24). The Puritan Matthew Henry holds both readings together: there was no other way, unless he would fetch a compass beyond Jordan — and yet "some think that Christ must needs go through Samaria because of the good work he had to do there; a poor woman to be converted, a lost sheep to be sought." It was happy for Samaria, he says, that it lay in Christ's way (Ezekiel 16:6).

The route also raises a harmony problem: had not Christ forbidden his disciples the Samaritan cities (Matthew 10:5)? Gill answers that necessity excuses passage, and that the prohibition concerned preaching there, not barely entering; Henry agrees — Christ neither preached publicly nor wrought a miracle in Samaria, his eye being on the lost sheep of the house of Israel, so that the kindness done here was, as it were, accidental: "a crumb of the children's bread that casually fell from the master's table." Henry also sketches who these Samaritans were — mongrel Jews in blood and religion, descendants of the Assyrian colonists mingled with the poor of the land, worshipping the God of Israel at their rival temple on Gerizim, claiming kindred with the Jews in prosperity and disowning them in distress (Ezra 4:2) — and the Antiochene Chrysostom tells the fuller story: transplanted Gentiles taught by a priest after God sent lions among them, who "only half changed," worshipping God yet keeping their idols, and taking their name from mount Somer. From the mixed company along that unavoidable road Henry draws his lesson: since the wicked are intermixed with God's Israel in this world (1 Corinthians 5:10), we cannot avoid passing through such places — but we should not reside in them; we should hasten through.
5:
Where exactly is Sychar? The older witnesses assume, and the newer dispute. Gill states the traditional identification without hesitation: Sychar is Sichem or Shechem, now called Neapolis. Clarke agrees and explains the strange form as a taunt — the name signifies "drunken," fastened on the town for the drunkenness of its Ephraimite inhabitants, the very charge Isaiah pressed (Isaiah 28:1); Calvin reports that Jerome thought "Sychar" a corrupted reading for Sichem, though he judges the altered name simply current in the Evangelist's day; Wesley notes without fuss that it was "formerly called Sichem or Shechem." But Ellicott mounts a sustained case for a different conclusion: John's careful prefix "which is called," the definition by the nearby parcel of ground, the fourth-century testimony of Eusebius, Jerome, and the Bordeaux Pilgrim distinguishing Sychar from Shechem, and the surviving village of Askar half a mile north of Jacob's well all argue that Sychar was a distinct place, not great Shechem under a nickname. Robertson leaves the question balanced — a variation meaning "drunken-town" (Isaiah 28:1) or "lying-town" (Habakkuk 2:18), or a separate village as the Talmud and Eusebius indicate — adding only that Askar "corresponds well with the site," and that John's word for city is used freely of small places.

On the parcel of ground the cloud speaks with one voice, layering the patriarchal records. Jacob bought a field there of the children of Hamor for a hundred pieces of silver (Genesis 33:19); dying, he gave Joseph a portion above his brethren, taken "out of the hand of the Amorite with my sword and with my bow" (Genesis 48:22); and there Joseph's bones were at last buried (Joshua 24:32). Lightfoot bridges purchase and conquest: after the slaughter of the Shechemites, Jacob's withdrawal let the Amorites thrust themselves into possession, and he was fain to regain his own ground by force. Ellicott hears in the Hebrew a deliberate play — the word for "portion" is itself Shechem — and Barnes rehearses the town's long biography: Abram's first altar in the land (Genesis 12:6-7), Joshua's covenant assembly (Joshua 24:1), Abimelech's destruction (Judges 9:45), Jeroboam's royal seat (1 Kings 12:25), and at last Roman Flavia Neapolis, corrupted into the modern Nablus. The Antiochene Chrysostom remembers a darker association: this was the place of Simeon and Levi's great slaughter for Dinah.

Only the fathers ask why the Evangelist bothers with these antiquities. Theophylact gives a double answer: to explain the woman's coming boast, "our father Jacob gave us this well," and to remind the reader that what the patriarchs obtained by faith the Jews lost by impiety — they were supplanted to make room for Gentiles, so there is nothing new in Gentiles now succeeding to the kingdom of heaven. The Latin Alcuin presses the figure a step further: the parcel was left not so much to Joseph as to Christ, of whom Joseph was a type, and to that ground the true heir now comes, that the Samaritans who claimed the patriarch's inheritance might recognize the patriarch's legal heir and be converted.
6:
Robertson opens with a curiosity of vocabulary: John calls this a spring of Jacob here and in v.14, but a well — a dug cistern — in vv.11-12; it is really a shaft a hundred feet deep, dug by a stranger in a land of abundant springs (Genesis 26:19). The site itself is among the least disputed in Palestine — Ellicott calls it one of the few spots about which all travellers agree, nine feet across, cut in the rock between Ebal and Gerizim. Clarke transcribes Maundrell's account of 1697: an old stone vault entered by a strait hole, the well three yards in diameter and thirty-five deep, five of them water — enough, Maundrell dryly notes, to confute the tale that it stands dry all year save on the anniversary of the Saviour's sitting there. Barnes adds the testimony of the missionary Eli Smith, who found it covered with a great pierced stone; Gill hunts it through the Talmud's "fountain of Sochar." Lightfoot even hears the well in Jacob's dying blessing, "Joseph is a fruitful bough by a well" (Genesis 49:22).

The picture of Jesus sitting thus draws out the chapter's first theology. Chrysostom, cited by Clarke, takes the little word to mean simply as he was — not on a throne or cushion but on the ground, on the first place he saw, wearied and waiting for his disciples; Lightfoot insists the Evangelist means us to know Christ begged water in good earnest, urged by real thirst and weariness. Ellicott dwells on the descriptive imperfect, "was sitting" — the fixed picture of an eyewitness — and on its weight: the fatigue is one of the standing witnesses to the reality of Christ's human nature. The Latin Augustine turns that into his famous antithesis: Jesus is strong and weak — strong, because in the beginning was the Word; weak, because the Word was made flesh. Calvin adds that he did not pretend weariness but was actually fatigued, taking our weaknesses upon him that he might be a high priest touched with the feeling of our infirmities (Hebrews 4:15). The Puritan Matthew Henry moralizes the scene: a true man, for toil came in with sin (Genesis 3:19); a poor man, who went all his journeys on foot; a tender man, it seems, since the disciples walked on to the town unwearied while their Master could not go a step further.

Over the sixth hour the schools genuinely divide. Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott read Jewish time: about noon, accounting for the heat, the thirst, and the disciples' errand at the ordinary dinner hour — and Ellicott notes that a solitary woman at the well suits an unusual, sun-scorched hour. Robertson reads Roman time, about six in the evening, "the usual time for drawing water"; Clarke records that Macknight had argued the same. The Latin fathers, meanwhile, count a different clock altogether: for Augustine and Alcuin the sixth hour is the sixth age of the world, from Adam to Noah down to the Baptist, in which the Word came wearied with his journey — that is, wearied with the flesh he assumed for our sake — and his sitting signifies either his humility or the posture of the teacher about to teach.
7:
The Antiochene Chrysostom begins defensively: lest the conversation seem to violate Christ's own charge against going to the Samaritans, the Evangelist shows how it arose — Jesus did not come intending to talk with the woman; he simply would not send her away when she came, and "she comes quite by chance." The Reformed Gill flatly reverses that last phrase: her coming was "not by chance, but by the providence of God," who orders all things by the counsel of his will; and he marvels at the choice — that a lewd and infamous Samaritan woman should be a chosen vessel of salvation while so many wise, learned, and religious men in Judea were passed by, and should then become the means of carrying the knowledge of the Saviour to her neighbours. The two are not so far apart as they sound — Chrysostom describes the surface of the encounter, Gill its decree — but the difference in instinct is real and worth marking.

The request itself the witnesses read on two levels at once. Theophylact insists the thirst was genuine: as man, the labour and heat had made him dry; Ellicott calls "give me to drink" the favour almost always asked and almost never refused where traveller meets native at a well-side. Yet Calvin holds both truths together with characteristic balance: thirst really prompted him to ask, but he preferred the woman's salvation to his own wants, and "forgetting his own thirst," watered with heavenly doctrine the mind of her who had refused him water. Augustine compresses the paradox into a sentence the tradition never forgot: he who asked to drink out of the woman's vessel "thirsted for the woman's faith" — he thirsts for the faith of all for whom he shed his blood. Barnes sees in the whole exchange a specimen of the remarkably happy manner in which Jesus could lead a conversation toward religion, and Wesley admires the compression from the other end: in this one conversation he brought her to a knowledge the apostles were long in attaining.

The grammatical-historical commentators fill in the frame. Robertson and Ellicott agree she is a woman of the country of Samaria, not of the city, which lay hours away; Robertson hears the vivid historical present — "there cometh" — and compares Rebekah at her well (Genesis 24:11); Clarke documents that drawing water was the women's employment from the patriarchs down (Exodus 2:16), adding from the rabbis that wells were where men sought wives — and where women of ill fame were also found. Augustine, characteristically, has already left the literal well behind: this woman is a type of the Church, not yet justified but about to be, and it belongs to the figure that she comes from a foreign people; for the Samaritans were foreigners though neighbours, and the Church was to come from the Gentiles, an alien to the Jewish race. Chrysostom draws a last, homelier lesson from the solitude that made the scene possible: the Lord of the whole earth was left alone, unattended, teaching fishermen newly raised to dignity to trample on every kind of pride.
8:
Lightfoot smells a problem in this innocent-looking parenthesis. If the disciples were gone into the city to buy food, how does that agree with the next verse's "Jews have no dealings with Samaritans" — and with the rabbinic rule he quotes, "Let no Israelite eat one mouthful of any thing that is a Samaritan's; for if he eat but a little mouthful, he is as if he ate swine's flesh"? He prints the famous anathema in full: Ezra, Zerubbabel, and Joshua gathering the congregation with three hundred priests, three hundred books of the law, and three hundred children to excommunicate the Samaritans — no morsel of their food, no proselytes from among them, no part for them in the resurrection. But he also finds the loophole in the Jerusalem Talmud itself: the victuals of the Cuthites are allowed, provided nothing of their wine or vinegar be mingled with them. Ellicott resolves the same tension historically: the strictest Jews allowed exceptions to the forbidden intercourse — if bread was interdicted, fruit and vegetables were not — and the Galileans probably never followed the Judean rigour in this matter at all; hence the disciples go off to a Samaritan market without scruple.

The grammatical-historical commentators attend to the small print. Robertson notes the past perfect — they had already gone before the woman arrived — and renders the purchase "victuals," in the plural; Ellicott corrects the old translation "meat" to "food," since the word covers herbs and fruits (Genesis 1:29-30) just as the meat-offering itself was flour and oil (Leviticus 2:1). On why the clause is here at all, the witnesses divide into a shallow and a deep reading. Wesley and Ellicott take it plainly: the disciples' absence explains the request — "else he needed not have asked her," for he was alone and had no means of drawing (John 4:11). The Reformed Gill thinks the Evangelist is showing something more deliberate: Christ took the opportunity of their absence to converse with her, partly to avoid the scandal and offence his disciples might take at his talking with a Samaritan — their astonishment when they returned proves the point (John 4:27) — and partly in mercy to the woman herself, that he might tell her the sins of her former life privately, and not put her to shame and blushing before them all.

The Antiochene Chrysostom reads the errand as a window on the Master's household economy: the disciples carried no store of food about with them, so careless was their Lord of provision; and his being left alone, unattended, was itself chosen — he could have kept some by him to wait on him, but he was training men of humble origin, suddenly raised to the loftiest station on earth, to subdue themselves in all things. The Puritan Matthew Henry adds his shrewd domestic notes: the meat Christ ate he bought and paid for (2 Thessalonians 3:8); he filled the vacant minutes while dinner was catering with the best work of the day, as Peter prayed while his meal was made ready (Acts 10:10); and he would teach us to comport with our circumstances, who could dine as contentedly beside a draw-well as in the best inn in the town.
9:
Her astonishment is the whole verse, and the witnesses hear its tone differently. The Reformed Gill hears "a scoffing, jeering way"; Calvin agrees — a reproach, retorting on Christ the contempt his nation held for hers: "Is it lawful for you to ask drink from me, when you hold us to be so profane?" — and he generalizes the reflex into a diagnosis: being desirous of esteem, we all take it ill to be despised, and this seed of pride lies in every mind till the Spirit of God eradicate it. Wesley, gentler, hears "open simplicity" in her very first words. Robertson reports Bernard's verdict that the astonishment is ironical, but adds his own homelier guess: perhaps she was simply surprised that a Jew would drink out of her waterpot. How she knew him for a Jew the commentators answer variously — his dress, says Clarke, or the Galilean dialect (Mark 14:70); the grammatical-historical Ellicott adds the delicious philological detail that the very request "Give me to drink" contained in Hebrew the shibboleth-sound the Ephraimites could never manage (Judges 12:5-6); Chrysostom says simply, from his figure and speech.

The explanatory clause — "for Jews have no dealings with Samaritans" — raises two questions. First, whose words are they? Clarke, Ellicott, and Robertson take them as the Evangelist's note for Greek readers, and Robertson observes that one family of manuscripts omits the parenthesis; Calvin thinks it more natural that the woman herself speaks the jeer, though he grants it is of little consequence which is preferred. Second, is the statement even true? Lightfoot the rabbinist protests that "no dealings" stretches the word: Jews merchandised with all the Gentile nations (Nehemiah 13:16), some rabbis pronounced the Samaritans "as the Israelites in all things," their unleavened bread was lawful, and an Israelite might circumcise a Cuthite — though not the reverse, "because he is circumcised into the name of mount Gerizim." The word means something narrower, a refusal of familiar communion: no borrowing, no common cup, no common table. Ellicott catalogues the curses that grew up around that refusal — the Samaritan's bread as swine's flesh, no proselytes, no share in the resurrection — and Clarke notes grimly that the Jews' most merciful wish for their neighbours was annihilation.

Behind the feud the witnesses sketch the history: Robertson calls the Samaritans a mixture by intermarriage of Jews left in the land with Shalmaneser's colonists (2 Kings 17:24), half Jews — which made the antipathy all the keener; Chrysostom notes they kept only the writings of Moses and made little of the Prophets. Calvin will not let the Jews off: there was just ground for separation from a corrupted worship, but for most of them zeal for the law was a cloak for carnal hatred, ambition, and envy. And Augustine catches the strangest note in the scene: the Jews would not so much as use Samaritan vessels — so the woman is astonished precisely because this Jew asks to drink from hers, while he, as Chrysostom says, asked what the custom disallowed on purpose, to show his indifference to such observances and to abolish them for the time to come.
10:
Ellicott reads this answer as a great reversal of roles, and states it more fully than anyone: she stands by the deep well that has been God's gift of refreshment for centuries, holding the means to draw, the apparent benefactor of the thirsty stranger — but it is not really so. There is a deeper well of spiritual truth in communion with God; he stands beside it with the power to enter depths hidden from man; and it is really she who is the traveller in the journey of life, weary with its burning heat, travel-stained by her sins, thirsting and helpless at the very side of the well — "for the Eternal is ever near you, and you know Him not." That is what "if thou knewest" means, and the grammatical-historical Robertson confirms it from the grammar: a condition determined as unfulfilled — she did not know, and so she did not ask.

What is the gift of God? Robertson answers with Westcott: naturally the gift of John 3:16, the inexpressible gift (2 Corinthians 9:15) — Christ himself; Clarke agrees, a free gift in the strictest sense, for Christ was not an object of desire to the world, no man asked for him, and God freely gave him; Barnes allows either Jesus himself or the opportunity of salvation then set before her. And the living water? Nearly all say the Spirit and his grace: Clarke points to John 7:38-39; Wesley calls it the Spirit and its fruits, noting that Jesus draws his allegories from water, bread, and light — the simplest, most necessary things in nature — and that the woman could the more easily mistake him because "living water" was the common phrase for spring water. Augustine had already made the physical definition do theological work: living water is what flows from a spring, in distinction from what is collected in ponds and cisterns; and even spring water, cut off from its fountain-head, ceases to be living. Robertson observes that Jacob's well was in fact just such a cistern, filled by percolating rains — good water, but never the equal of a true spring (Genesis 26:19). Chrysostom explains why Scripture can call the one grace both fire and water: the names describe not its essence but its operation — fire for the rousing, sin-consuming energy, water for the cleansing and the great refreshment.

Two voices press the verse into doctrine. The Alexandrian Origen finds in "thou wouldest have asked" a standing law of grace: no one receives a divine gift who does not seek it — even the Son is bidden, "Ask of me, and I will give thee" (Psalm 2:8). Calvin turns the scene into a model of unmerited calling: what was there in this wretched woman, that from a prostitute she should suddenly become a disciple of the Son of God, while so many great men in Judea were passed by? It is Isaiah's word made visible: "I was found by them that sought me not" (Isaiah 65:1). And the knowledge of the gift, he adds, begins with the knowledge of our poverty — the Lord invites not the satiated but the thirsty; why should Christ come with the fullness of the Spirit, if we were not empty?
11:
"Sir," she says — and on that one word the witnesses part company. Ellicott hears a change of key: something in his voice and manner has touched her; she does not understand his words but is conscious of their latent force, and the pert "Thou, being a Jew" gives way to a reverential address to One who teaches with authority. Chrysostom likewise says these words raised her notions of the speaker and made her think him no common person. The Reformed voices will have none of it. Gill calls the title the usual civility of the country, expressing "no uncommon respect," and insists she is still scoffing; Calvin reads the whole reply as mockery — as the Jews despised the Samaritans, so the Samaritans held the Jews in contempt, and this woman "not only disdains Christ but even mocks at him." She understands quite well that he speaks figuratively, Calvin thinks, and answers jibe for jibe, meaning that he promises more than he can perform. Lightfoot goes furthest: she "laughs in her sleeve," and her words are scorn, not supplication.

Her objection, at least, is perfectly practical, and the grammatical-historical school lays out its logic. Barnes explains that no drawing-gear was fixed at the well; every traveller carried pail and cord, and Jesus manifestly had neither. Clarke fills in the eastern picture — leathern buckets and a line were part of a traveller's equipment, and if the shaft was then, as Maundrell found it, thirty-five yards deep, no short line would serve. Robertson adds the exact furniture of the scene: the word she uses means the rope or vessel for drawing; the well is a hundred feet deep; the skin bucket with its cross-sticks was ordinarily kept at the well and let down by a goat's hair rope. He also notes the broken "neither — and" construction, found elsewhere in the New Testament only at 3 John 1:10, as if her sentence stumbled between two thoughts: you have no bucket — and the well is deep.

Her phrase "that living water" shows where her mind still is. Ellicott observes that she thinks of spring water, the "living water" of the Hebrew scriptures (Genesis 26:19; Leviticus 14:5), and that John quietly changes his word here: in v.6 the well was named from its spring, but in her mouth it is the word for a deep pit (Luke 14:5) — the depth is now the point. Robertson agrees: "the water, the living," with the article, picks up Jesus' own phrase from v.10, but she is still thinking only of literal water. Whence, then, can he have it? The question is meant to corner him — and, as Augustine saw, it opens directly into the comparison she reaches for next: he has nothing to draw with as she has, so he can only make good his promise by being greater than the giver of the well.
12:
"Our father Jacob" — the boast, all the witnesses agree, is the key to the verse, and most of them dispute her right to it. Gill notes that the Samaritans claimed Jacob "when it served their turn," being in truth the planted peoples of Babylon, Cuthah, Ava, Hamath, and Sepharvaim, later salted with renegades who followed the deposed priest to Sanballat's temple on Gerizim — "a mixed medley of people," who nevertheless draw their genealogy from Abraham to this day. Clarke concedes more history: the ancient Samaritans really were the ten revolted tribes, but by our Lord's time a corrupted race sprung of Shalmaneser's mixture (2 Kings 17:24). Wesley is dry: "so they fancied." Robertson notes the claim ran through Joseph and Ephraim, and that her question with its negative particle expects the answer no — thou surely art not greater than Jacob. Chrysostom smiles at how "she thrusts herself upon the Jewish stock"; Bede alone offers a charitable gloss — perhaps she calls Jacob father because she lived under the law of Moses and dwelt on Jacob's own ground.

Her commendation of the well the witnesses read as ancestral pride in miniature. Ellicott reconstructs its force: the well that satisfied the patriarch, his household, and his flocks has come down to us and suffices for all our wants — sacred as the one unbroken continuity in a land whose people had none. Theophylact notices the workmanlike detail "and his cattle": the water was not only sweet enough for Jacob and his sons but abundant enough for the herds of a patriarchal household. Barnes hears the universal human note — men the world over commend what their ancestors used, and boast of inherited honours, even when the inheritance is nothing better than water Jacob's cattle drank — and he adds that her supposing Jesus meant some rival spring shows how ready sinners are to misunderstand Christ and pervert the doctrines of religion.

Calvin turns the verse into a warning about misplaced veneration. The comparison is faulty at the root, he says: she compares the servant to the master, a dead man to the living God — "and yet how many in the present day fall into this very error?" We ought to honour men eminent in piety, but in such a manner that God remain eminent above all, and that Christ with his Gospel outshine every human splendor. He presses the polemical parallel: as the Samaritans, Cutheans by descent, falsely boasted of the holy patriarchs, so the Papists, "a bastard seed," arrogantly boast of the Fathers and despise the true children of God — and such boasting profits nothing, for those who exult in the light of men deprive themselves of the light of God. The Alexandrian Origen, characteristically, has meanwhile allegorized the whole scene: Jacob's well is the Scriptures; the learned drink of it like Jacob and his sons, the simple and uneducated like Jacob's cattle — a mystical turn no other witness in the cloud follows him into here.
13:
The Antiochene Chrysostom notices what Jesus does not say. To her challenge, "Art thou greater than our father Jacob?" he does not reply "I am greater," lest he seem to boast — but his answer implies it: if Jacob is honoured for giving you this water, what will you say if I give you far better? And the comparison is made, Chrysostom insists, without a word against the patriarch: Jesus does not call the well's water vile or counterfeit, he asserts a simple fact of nature — whoever drinks of this water shall thirst again. Barnes makes the same observation from the modern side: no direct answer to her question, no slight upon Jacob or his gift, only a premise from which she might draw the inference herself, since however excellent the well, its water "could not altogether remove thirst." Gill pauses over the manner before the matter: Jesus answers "in a mild and gentle manner, patiently bearing all her scoffs and flouts," and simply continues to instruct her. Robertson finds the theology hidden in the tenses: the present participle of the first clause describes one who must keep on drinking — Jesus pointing at the well — while the coming promise of v.14 uses the aorist, a drinking once for all.

What is this water that cannot satisfy? For the literalists it is simply well-water, and the saying a plain observation about the body's recurring want. The Latin Augustine takes it also as a type: the water in the well is the pleasure of the world, that abode of darkness; men draw it with the waterpot of their lusts, for pleasure is not relished unless lust go before it; and having drunk, they thirst again — but they shall not thirst "who are drunken with the abundance of the house of God." Calvin, without the allegory, reaches the same doctrine by analysis: Christ distinguishes the two waters by their uses — the one serves the body, and only for a time, since the body itself is decaying and its supports must be frail and transitory; the other gives perpetual vigour to the soul, and what quickens the soul cannot but be eternal.

Calvin also meets the obvious objection: do not believers, to the very end of life, burn with desire for more grace — do they not thirst? Yes, he answers, and keenly, their whole life long; but the promise is not that from the first day we drink to satiety. It is that the Holy Spirit is "a continually flowing fountain," so that there is no danger that they who have been renewed by spiritual grace shall ever be dried up; however small the measure received, it gives perpetual vigour. The desire of the saints is longing, not languishing — thirst within abundance. In the same spirit Chrysostom, preaching on the passage, explains why Scripture can call this one grace now fire and now water (Matthew 3:11; John 7:38): the names describe operations, not essence — and the water-name declares the cleansing and the great refreshment of grace, which makes the willing soul like a garden thick with fruitful, ever-flourishing trees, and quenches all the fiery darts of the wicked one.
14:
Wesley's paraphrase catches the crescendo of the promise: not barely a well, which is soon exhausted, but a fountain — an inward living principle — "springing up into everlasting life, which is a confluence, or rather an ocean of streams arising from this fountain." He adds his characteristic Wesleyan proviso: the believer will never thirst provided he continue to drink; if ever that thirst returns, it will be the fault of the man, not of the water. Barnes builds the image outward into the eastern landscape: the soul by nature is a desert traveller, thirsting for happiness and trying every object in vain, to whom the grace of Christ is as cold waters to a thirsty soul; and the fountain within is no stagnant pool but a spring that flows in heat and cold, prosperity and adversity, persecution, contempt, or death — eternal in its nature and its supply. Clarke forges the links of a chain: no eternal life without the Spirit, no Spirit without Christ, and no Christ giving the Spirit without dwelling in the heart.

The grammatical-historical observations tighten the sense. Robertson notes the double negative — the strongest denial the language allows, "shall never, no never, thirst" (its twin construction stands in John 6:35) — and the rare participle, found again only in the leaping of the healed cripples (Acts 3:8; Acts 14:10): water that leaps up, bubbling toward eternal life. He adds that Jesus has still not answered the woman's question about Jacob save by necessary implication: only one greater than Jacob could promise such water. Ellicott states the contrast the emphatic words carry: not an external supply, sought again at every recurrence of want, but an inner never-failing source which, coming from the fountain of all life, issues in eternal life (John 7:38).

The older voices supply the pneumatology. Gill identifies the water without hesitation: the Spirit and his grace, Christ's own gift. Cyril of Jerusalem, catechizing on this very text, calls it "a new kind of water, living and springing up, springing up unto them who are worthy," and reads it by John 7:39 — this spake the Saviour of the Spirit, which they that believe on him should receive; the rivers it feeds are not rivers perceived by sense, watering thorns and trees, but rivers bringing souls to the light. Theophylact adds a note on cooperation congenial to the East: the water is "ever multiplying" — the saints receive through grace the seed and principle of good, but they themselves make it grow by their own cultivation. Calvin's summary gathers it all: the words denote a continual watering which maintains in believers a heavenly eternity during this mortal and perishing life; the grace of Christ does not flow to us for a short time but overflows into a blessed immortality, and does not cease to flow until the incorruptible life it begins has been brought to perfection.
15:
Jest or earnest? The Puritan Matthew Henry confesses the request is "hard to say" which: some think she speaks tauntingly, ridiculing what Christ had said and challenging him in derision — "a rare invention; it will save me a great deal of pains" — while others take it for a well-meant but weak and ignorant desire, an Amen said at a venture to something she perceived was good. The cloud divides along just that line. Calvin is severest: she is sufficiently aware that Christ speaks of spiritual water, but because she despises him she sets all his promises at naught — "Thou boastest much, but I see nothing: show it in reality, if thou canst" — for so long as the authority of the speaker is not acknowledged, his doctrine cannot enter. Lightfoot agrees that her answer is scoff, not supplication, and Gill had already heard mockery in her "Sir." Robertson splits the difference from the grammar: she speaks "half ironically," not grasping the phrase unto life eternal, and yet is "evidently puzzled and yet attracted" — she wants never to keep on coming, as she now must once or twice every day.

The Antiochene and Latin readings are kinder, seeing not scorn but slow ascent. Chrysostom traces the degrees by which she is being led toward the highest doctrine: first she thought him some lax Jew; then she took the living water for material water; now she understands it spiritually, believes it can take away thirst, and — remarkably — prefers him to the patriarch Jacob for whom she had such veneration, though she does not yet know what the gift is. Augustine reads her as still wholly carnal but sincerely eager: delighted at the thought of being relieved for ever from thirst, she remembers, as it were, that God once granted Elijah to hunger and thirst not for forty days — and if forty days, why not for ever? Her poverty obliged her to labour beyond her strength; would that she could hear, Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden (Matthew 11:28) — for Jesus had in fact been saying that very thing, and she did not understand him. Barnes draws the general lesson: her dullness shows how slow sinners are to understand the doctrines of religion; Clarke, more hopefully, sees the design succeeding — her curiosity was now thoroughly excited, and that was precisely what our Lord intended, to prepare her mind for the great truths he was about to announce. Ellicott meanwhile disposes of a textual curiosity: some old manuscripts read "come all the way hither," which Godet used to argue the woman lived elsewhere and passed the well from work — a conjecture, he judges, built on an uncertain reading and a more than uncertain rendering.
16:
Go, call thy husband looks, on its face, like a change of subject, and Calvin meets the suspicion head-on: one might suppose that Christ, annoyed and put to shame by the woman's impudence, changes the discourse. Not so, says the Reformed commentator. Perceiving that jeers and scoffs were her only reply, Jesus applies the appropriate remedy to the disease and strikes her conscience with a conviction of her sin; and it is a remarkable proof of his compassion that, when the woman would not come to him of her own accord, he draws her, as it were, against her will. Calvin then widens the point into a rule of the school of Christ: the utterly careless and almost stupid must be deeply wounded, or the doctrine of Christ remains to them a fable; the oil of Christ, when it has no flavour, must be mixed with wine that its taste may be felt; the earth must be softened by the ploughshare before it can bear fruit — and this is necessary not for scoffers only, for none of us is seriously affected by Christ speaking until we have been aroused by repentance.

The grammatical-historical witnesses read the same command as deliberate method rather than rupture. Barnes admires the manner our Saviour took: since she did not understand his instructions, he shows her that he is acquainted with her life and with her sins — a delicate and yet pungent way of making her see that she was a sinner — and by the same stroke proves himself qualified to teach her the way to heaven, preparing her to own him the Messiah (John 4:29). Clarke condenses it to two purposes: to make the woman consider her own state, and to show her that he knew her heart and the secret actions of her life. Ellicott gives the method its most memorable figure: she has asked for the living water, not knowing that the well must first be dug — down through many a hard rock of impenitence, many a layer of every-day transgression, many a habit once formable as clay and now hard as adamant. The command go, call thy husband is the first stroke breaking up the fair surface and revealing the foulness of the life beneath it.

Robertson attends to the two imperatives of the Greek and allows himself two conjectures: had she started to leave after her perplexed reply, and were her frequent trips to the well partly for her husband's sake? We may not have all the conversation preserved, he grants; but the point of the sudden sharp turn is not in doubt — Jesus gives the woman a conviction of sin and guilt without which she cannot understand his use of water as a metaphor for eternal life. Gill traces the same pastoral logic: observing that she continued an ignorant scoffer at him and his words, Christ determines to take another method, to convince her that he is no common person and that she, a sinner of vicious life, stands in need of him as the gift of God and Saviour of men. The Wesleyan founder says it shortest: Jesus now clears the way, that he might give her a better kind of water than she asked for — and in go, call thy husband 'he strikes directly at her bosom sin.'
17:
Her answer is only four words — I have no husband — and most of the witnesses observe that its truth is made to do the work of concealment. Barnes says plainly that it was spoken to evade the subject: perhaps she feared that if the man she lived with were called, the truth might be exposed; and it is not improbable that by this time she began to suspect that Jesus was a prophet. Robertson roots the evasion in the Greek: the word means either 'man' or 'husband'; she had her 'man,' but he was no legal husband, and 'her language veils her deceit.' Jesus sees through the double sense and reads her heart — that supernatural knowledge of which this Gospel so often speaks (John 1:48; John 2:24-25) — and Robertson hears precision even in the commendation: not the simple 'thou saidst' but the perfect, 'this hast thou said truly.' Ellicott listens to the tone: the reply is prefaced no longer by the half-sarcastic 'thou, being a Jew,' nor even by the reverential 'Sir'; vivacity has passed into earnestness, and earnestness into sadness. The stroke has left its mark. That one word — what a history it has revealed! Her life lies bare to her own consciousness, though she does not know that it lies bare to his; and the Sinaitic manuscript, he notes, makes her denial more emphatic still: 'A husband I have not.'

Gill is the one witness who lets the answer stand as simple candour. This was a truth, he says, that she would not have spoken at another time and place, or to any of her neighbours; but Christ was a stranger, no odium would fall upon her by it, and it served a purpose, excusing her from going to call anyone — so 'she declares the truth of the matter.' The Reformed Calvin, by contrast, presses into what that truth contains. We are so intoxicated, or rather stupefied, by self-love that the first wounds inflicted do not move us at all; Christ therefore cures the sluggishness by pressing the ulcer more sharply, openly reproaching her with her wickedness. And Calvin ventures a reconstruction of her history that none of the others share: this is not a single case of fornication but the record of a froward and disobedient wife who constrained her husbands to divorce her — 'though God joined thee to lawful husbands, thou didst not cease to sin, until, rendered infamous by numerous divorces, thou prostitutedst thyself to fornication.'

The grammatical-historical gloss on 'thou hast well said' is the plainest possible — 'hast said the truth,' says Barnes — while the Wesleyan John Wesley draws from the strange commendation a rule of Christ's whole manner of speech: 'we may observe in all our Lord's discourses the utmost weightiness, and yet the utmost courtesy.' The heaviest reproof in the chapter is about to fall; it opens by commending the one true thing her evasion said.
18:
How guilty was she? Nowhere in the passage do the witnesses divide more openly. Lightfoot, grammatical-historical in school but rabbinic in method, takes the darkest reading: Christ 'stops her fleering mouth with the dung of her own unchaste conversation,' charging that for her impudent adulteries she has suffered divorce from five husbands already, and that he whom she now has is no husband but an adulterer. He argues it from the extraordinary number, and from Jesus naming the husbands along with the present companion, as if she had lived dishonestly under them too; and he adds from his rabbinic store that 'the Cuthites do not understand the law about betrothings and divorcings' — though their very inexactness, he grants, may have kept their divorces nearer Moses' first institution, which allowed none but for adultery. Gill inclines the same way: she might have lawfully buried five, as the Sadducees could pose a case of seven; 'or rather,' he judges, she had been divorced from every one of them for adultery — for no other cause, it seems, did the Samaritans divorce.

Clarke will have none of it. It is not clear, he insists, that this woman was a prostitute. She might have been legally married to all five and divorced for misbehaviour not amounting to adultery — for the adulteress, by both Jewish and Samaritan law, was to be put to death, not divorced — or cast off through mere caprice, divorces being very common in that age (Matthew 5:31). He mocks the exaggerators: Heracleon gives her six husbands, and Jerome 'modestly' twenty-two. And he reports Bishop Pearce's bolder defence, that he whom thou now hast is not thy husband may mean only that she was contracted to a man but not yet brought home, so that the words carry no reproof at all: a woman old enough to have had five husbands is unlikely now to be living in adultery with a sixth; our Lord nowhere else in the discourse reproves her fornication; a woman of scandalous life could hardly have carried her whole city on her testimony (John 4:39-42); nor would such a person likely be the first private hearer of Christ's open declaration (John 4:26).

Between these poles stand the rest. Barnes lays the possibilities flatly side by side — the five may have died, or divorced her for improper conduct, or been left improperly without legal divorce — but on the present connection he does not waver: she is not lawfully married to the man, whether through an undissolved former bond or open cohabitation. Ellicott holds both histories in a single sentence — 'the sad days of death, which five times over had robbed her of a husband; or, worse than death, the sin which had severed the sacred bonds' — and then the stroke goes deeper, laying bare the years she thought veiled for ever, and proving Christ to know all the acts of her life (John 4:29). With the loss of purity, he observes, is linked the loss of truthfulness: her very truth was meant to convey an untruth, yet to One who knows all, the words are really true — in that saidst thou truly. The Wesleyan founder closes in one line: whether the five were all dead or not, 'her own conscience now awakened would tell her.'
19:
Chrysostom is struck first by what the woman does not do. She is not offended at Christ's rebuke; she does not leave him and go away — far from it, her admiration for him rises: 'Your knowledge of me is unaccountable; you must be a prophet.' And having come to that belief, the Antiochene notes, she asks nothing that relates to this life — no question of the health or sickness of the body, no more trouble about thirst — 'she is eager for doctrine.' Augustine, from the Latin side of the Catena, carries forward his allegory of the summoned husband: the husband — the understanding — 'was beginning to come to her, though he had not yet fully come.' She thought our Lord a prophet, and, Augustine adds, so he was, for he says of himself that a prophet is not without honour save in his own country.

The moderns fix on what the word prophet meant on her lips. For Barnes it denotes here not a foreteller of future events but one who knew her heart and life and must therefore have come from God; she did not yet suppose him to be the Messiah (John 4:25). Robertson lingers on the verb: 'I am beginning to perceive' — perception dawning from his knowledge of her private life (v.29), with the emphasis of the Greek falling on thou. Ellicott turns it into her own inward question: who can it be who thus enters her mind and reads the pages of her memory as if it were a book? He must be as one of those of olden time of whom she has heard — and the tone of reverence prevails again: 'Sir.' Gill catches the change in her bearing: another countenance, a different air and gesture, another accent and tone of speech, 'dropping her scoffs and jeers'; Clarke adds that she now thought him well qualified to decide the grand question in dispute between the Jews and the Samaritans, though she did not perceive him to be the Messiah.

What the confession leads to is disputed. Barnes takes the low view: the conversation about her manner of life was very unpleasant — as talk of their lives always is to sinners — and she was glad to turn it to something else; nothing is more common, he moralizes, than for sinners under conviction to change the subject to some speculative inquiry with a connection to religion, willing to talk about religion so long as it is not about themselves. Robertson agrees: she wanted to change the subject at once to the outstanding theological dispute. The Reformed Calvin reads the same movement in the opposite direction: here is 'the fruit of the reproof' — not only does the woman modestly acknowledge her fault, but, ready and prepared, she now desires and requests of her own accord the doctrine she had formerly disdained. Repentance is the commencement of true docility, and opens the gate for entering the school of Christ; and her example teaches us to seize the opportunity of the teacher God sends, for how shall they preach unless they are sent (Romans 10:15)? Wesley sides with him in six words: 'So soon was her heart touched.'
20:
Gerizim stood in sight as she spoke. Gill notes that the mountain was so near that the woman could point to it — near enough that Jotham's voice once carried from its top to the city (Judges 9:7) — Robertson sets Jacob's Well at its very foot, and Clarke pictures her 'probably pointing' as she speaks. The grammatical-historical witnesses then unpack the claim compressed into our fathers: Abraham built an altar in this district (Genesis 12:6-7), Jacob raised at Shechem the altar he called El-elohe-Israel (Genesis 33:18-20), and the blessing was set upon Gerizim itself (Deuteronomy 11:29). There Sanballat built the rival temple — Wesley and Clarke tell the story of Manasseh, expelled from the priesthood for marrying Sanballat's daughter (Nehemiah 13:28) — and John Hyrcanus destroyed it about 129 B.C., though, Robertson observes, a handful of Samaritans keep up the worship on the mountain still.

Behind the claim most of the moderns find a corrupted text: where the Hebrew of Deuteronomy 27:4 commands the stones to be set up in mount Ebal, the Samaritan Pentateuch reads Gerizim — 'notoriously falsified,' says Lightfoot; part of the ground of their error, says Barnes; though Clarke fairly reports that Dr. Kennicott 'strongly contends' the Samaritan reading is the genuine one. Lightfoot adds the Talmud's satire on the mountain's sanctity: the Samaritan who urged a rabbi bound for Jerusalem to pray rather in this holy mountain than 'that cursed house,' and who, asked whence the holiness came, answered that the mountain was not overflown by the waters of the deluge — 'a doughty reason indeed!' From the Catena, Augustine stages the dispute itself — the Jews boasting of Solomon's temple, the Samaritans retorting: did our fathers, who pleased God, worship in that temple, or in this mountain? Chrysostom says that by our fathers she means Abraham, said to have offered up Isaac here — a tradition Gill flatly brands 'entirely false' — and the Alexandrian Origen distils the quarrel into etymology: Gerizim, 'division,' befits the Samaritans, type of heretics; Sion, 'watch-tower,' the Jews, type of true believers.

Her motive divides the cloud. Ellicott is certain the question of worship was not the engrossing problem of her mind — such questions hardly came within the circle of a Samaritan woman's thoughts, and her life had not made her the exception; the heart, quivering before the eye that reads it as it never before had read itself, shrinks from the light, and 'she will speak of anything rather than of self.' Robertson concurs: by raising the famous wrangle she would turn the attention of Jesus away from herself. The Reformed Calvin calls that 'a mistaken opinion': she passes rather from the particular to the general and, having been informed of her sin, wishes to be instructed generally concerning the pure worship of God — for nothing is more wicked than to contrive modes of worship without the word of God; and Wesley agrees that she proposes 'what she thought the most important of all questions.' Calvin then turns the Samaritans' plea from the fathers into his long discourse on false imitation: the patriarchs might lawfully build altars everywhere while the place was yet unfixed, but once God chose Zion that liberty ceased (Deuteronomy 12:8-14) — so Bethel itself became Beth-aven — and examples borrowed from the saints, he warns, have gone on burying the church under ceremonies ever since.
21:
Wesley notices that our Lord uses believe me in this manner only once in all his recorded speech — and uses it to a Samaritan; to his own people, the Jews, the usual language is 'I say unto you.' Robertson makes the same observation from the text: the unique phrase stands where the double 'verily' usually stands, and it introduces what he does not hesitate to call one of Christ's greatest truths, told to this sinful woman. The Antiochene Chrysostom, whose homily here is preserved at length, hears in it the demand for the one indispensable thing: faith, 'the mother of blessings, the medicine of salvation,' without which men attempting the great doctrines are like swimmers crossing the open sea without a ship — holding out a little way with hands and feet, then swamped by the waves. This is the sacred anchor by which Christ brings the Samaritan woman over; and he marks the astonishing privilege — an exceedingly great doctrine is revealed to her which was mentioned neither to Nicodemus nor to Nathanael. Christ declines her either/or: he will not explain why the fathers worshipped in the mountain and the Jews at Jerusalem, but takes away from both places their priority in dignity. From the Latin fathers, Alcuin reads the hour cometh as the Gospel dispensation now approaching, under which the shadows of types withdraw and the pure light of truth enlightens the minds of believers; Augustine, still pursuing his figure, finds 'believe me' fitly said now that the husband — the believing understanding — has begun to be present in her.

The moderns map what is being abolished and what is not. Barnes is careful with the negative: Christ does not say there will be no worship of God at Jerusalem or here, but that worship will not be confined to either — celebrated everywhere, with as much acceptance in one place as another (Malachi 1:11; Matthew 18:20). Calvin says the ceremonial worship appointed under the Law is here briefly set aside: what Moses delivered was only for a time, and the hour approaches when the partition-wall (Ephesians 2:14) shall be thrown down, the worship of God extended far beyond its former narrow limits, that the Samaritans might become partakers of it. Ellicott punctuates the sentence so that its promise stands clear — 'when ye shall, neither in this mountain nor yet in Jerusalem, worship the Father' — worship shall go on, only without the limitation of holy places; and he catches the tenses of her own speech: she had referred to Gerizim's worship in the past, its temple more than a century and a half in ruins, but to Jerusalem's in the present — while he who had just come from that temple carried its coming ruin already in his thoughts (John 2:18-22).

Nearly every witness lingers on the chosen name. Clarke: the Father shows the mild, benignant, and tender nature of the Gospel dispensation, whose own prayer begins 'Our Father.' Calvin hears a pointed contrast with the 'fathers' the woman had pleaded: God will be a common Father to all, generally worshipped without distinction of places or nations. Ellicott widens it furthest — the Father of mankind, before whom Jew, Samaritan, and Gentile are brethren. And Gill grounds the whole appeal in what she has just confessed: believe me in what I am now going to say, 'since you own me to be a prophet.'
22:
No verse in the discourse has been fought over from the outside like this one. Salvation is of the Jews, reports the grammatical-historical Ellicott, 'has sorely tried critics who seek to construct the Gospel out of their judgments of what it should be': assume a Greek Gospel of the late second century and the clause must go, 'though it is certainly part of the original text'; accept a Hebrew Gospel of the first century and this touch of Jewish theology is in entire harmony — the Jews, little as they knew the treasure they possessed, were the guardians of spiritual truth for the world (Romans 3:2; Isaiah 2:3). Robertson refuses to soften the scandal: however unworthy the Jews may have proved of their privilege, 'this tremendous fact should never be forgotten' — the Messiah, God's Son, was a Jew (Matthew 15:24).

What the Samaritans' ignorance consisted in divides the cloud. Chrysostom takes the harder line: they worshipped 'a local, a partial God,' of whom they had the same notion that they had of their idols, and so mingled the worship of God with the worship of idols. Gill traces the pedigree — heathen colonists planted by the king of Assyria, who 'knew not the manner of the God of the land' till lions were sent among them, and who served every family its own graven images; Wesley cites the verdict of 2 Kings 17:33: they feared the Lord, but at the same time served their own gods. Clarke dissents in part: the Samaritans believed in the same God with the Jews, and after Sanballat's temple rose on Gerizim the idolatrous worship of the Cutheans 'was entirely laid aside'; the defect lay rather in rejecting all the prophetical writings. Robertson stands near him, quoting Westcott — 'you know whom to worship, but you do not know him' (compare Acts 17:23) — the rejection of prophets and Psalms cutting them off from the fuller knowledge of God.

On we know what we worship, Chrysostom observes that Christ reckons himself among the Jews in condescension to the woman's idea of him, speaking as if a Jewish prophet, 'though it is certain that he is the Being who is worshipped by all'; Augustine adds that he speaks not for the reprobate Jews but for that party from whom the apostles and the prophets came. The 'salvation' itself splits them gently. Clarke and Barnes read it of the Saviour in person — the Messiah who was to spring from the Jews (Luke 2:30; Acts 4:12) — which is how the woman appears to take it (John 4:25). Chrysostom takes it at full breadth: everything calculated to save and amend the world, the knowledge of God, the abhorrence of idols, the very origin of our religion, comes from the Jews, and in 'salvation' Christ includes his own presence in the flesh. The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrinal edge: unless there be knowledge, it is not God that we worship, but a phantom or idol — 'all good intentions, as they are called, are struck by this sentence, as by a thunderbolt' — for the Jews had certainty from the word of God, and all who forsake the word fall into idolatry. And the Alexandrian Origen makes ye all who understand the Scriptures in an heretical sense, and we the Word himself with all who are conformed to his image.
23:
Everything turns on three added words. In v.21 it was only 'the hour cometh'; now it is the hour cometh, and now is — and the grammatical-historical Robertson explains why the addition could not come earlier: local worship was not yet abolished, but spiritual independence of place was called for at once. Ellicott agrees: a band of true worshippers was already gathering, some then following him. Chrysostom hears the difference from old prophecy — no prediction awaiting the course of ages: 'the event is now at hand, it is approaching your very doors.' The Alexandrian Origen alone finds two hours: the first, named without 'and now is,' a purely spiritual worship suited only to perfection; the second, earthly worship perfected as far as human nature bears; and his allegory runs on to a Jerusalem which is the Church, itself destined to pass — 'the angels do not worship the Father at Jerusalem' — until, accounted sons, we hear not 'worship God' but 'worship the Father.'

What in spirit means is a quieter but real division. For Barnes the word stands opposed to rites and ceremonies and the pomp of external worship: it is the mind, the soul, the heart. Calvin agrees on the substance — worship is nothing else than the inward faith of the heart producing prayer, with purity of conscience and self-denial — and meets the objection himself: did not the fathers under the Law worship spiritually? They did — God, always like himself, never approved any other worship; the Law's worship was spiritual in substance but carnal in form, wrapped in shadows, and now the veil is rent (Matthew 27:51) — whence his polemic that Popery has overturned the distinction, its shadows thicker than the Jewish. Others hear the Holy Spirit in the word: Clarke — acceptable worship 'must spring from the heart, through the influence of the Holy Ghost'; Robertson — in the spirit of man, indeed, but all according to the Holy Spirit (Romans 8:5), the Spirit of truth (John 16:13); Ellicott — the human spirit is the shrine of the Holy Spirit (1 Corinthians 6:19), place, time, words, and postures aiding worship only so far as they lift the spirit, hindering the moment they distract. Cyril of Jerusalem cites the verse catechetically among the Saviour's own words concerning the Holy Ghost — proof 'that we ought to worship God in the Spirit.'

The last clause draws the tenderest comment. Chrysostom defines the 'true worshippers' by their opposites — the false, 'who pray for temporal and frail benefits, or whose actions are ever contradicting their prayers' — the old dispensation of types being condescension to human frailty. Barnes: God himself desires this worship, having appointed the old forms only to lead the mind toward himself. Origen: if the Father seeks, he seeks through Jesus, who came to seek and save the lost. Ellicott ends with the name Christ chose: not Vengeance to be appeased nor Power to be dreaded, but Love to be received — and the seeking is not human only, for the Father also seeks his child and sees him a great way off (Luke 15:20). Gill only insists the door is wide: true worshippers 'whether Jews or Samaritans, or of whatsoever nation'; and Wesley: not here or there only, but 'at all times and in all places.'
24:
God is Spirit, Robertson insists — not 'a Spirit': the sentence stands beside 'God is light' (1 John 1:5) and 'God is love' (1 John 4:8), and can no more be read backwards; three words state for the first time both the non-corporeality and the personality of God. Ellicott renders it likewise — 'better, God is spirit' — and gives it a local edge: the Samaritans themselves had altered Pentateuch passages that ascribed human form and feelings to God, so Christ appeals to a doctrine prominent among them, which answers of itself both place and mode of worship. And here, Robertson adds, is the real must — not the woman's necessity of place (v.20) but the necessity of God's own nature.

What the axiom proves divides the Reformed school against itself. Gill reads it as widely as possible: perhaps 'the Spirit is God' — a divine person, his deity shown by names, works, and worship — or, as most versions, 'God is a spirit,' no person excluded, the description agreeing with Father, Son, and Holy Ghost alike; and he unfolds the metaphysics: simple, uncompounded, indivisible, 'an immense and infinite spirit' without beginning or end. Calvin pulls the opposite way: the Fathers quoted this passage against the Arians to prove the divinity of the Holy Spirit, 'but it is improper to strain it for such a purpose; for Christ simply declares here that his Father is of a spiritual nature.' For Calvin the sentence grounds worship: men, being flesh, delight in what matches their disposition; but God can no more agree with the flesh than fire with water — what pleases us most is the object of his loathing.

The Catena's fathers each carry the word somewhere different. Chrysostom: the incorporeal God is worshipped not with the body but with the soul's pure mind — the Jews washed the body and neglected the soul. Origen: God is Spirit as constituting our real life, 'just as our breath constitutes our bodily life.' Hilary: a Spirit worshipped in spirit means freedom, knowledge, and worship uncircumscribed — where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is liberty. Theophylact guards the second term: many worship with the mind yet hold heretical doctrines of God — hence 'and in truth'; unless spirit be action and truth contemplation, or the whole a stroke against Samaritan place and Jewish type. Augustine turns doctrine into prayer: you cry for a high mountain, that God who dwells on high may hear you better — but he has respect to the humble: 'descend, that you may ascend'; would you pray in the temple? 'Pray in yourself; but first become the temple of God.'

The rest draw the practical inference. Barnes: the bodiless, invisible, omnipresent God dwells not in temples made with hands (Acts 7:48) and needs nothing (Acts 17:25); he seeks the offering of the soul rather than of the body. Clarke calls the saying 'one of the greatest, most sublime, and necessary truths in the compass of nature,' and — alone in the cloud — corroborates it from the East, from Abu'l Fazel on worship in holiness of spirit and Creeshna's Geeta on the soul possessed of God's spirit. The Wesleyan founder ends: a Spirit remote from body yet full of all spiritual perfections — power, wisdom, love, holiness — 'and our worship should be suitable to his nature,' animating all our tempers, thoughts, words, and actions.
25:
A single various reading opens Adam Clarke's note: several excellent manuscripts turn the woman's I know into we know, as though she spoke for all Samaria, where the tradition of the Messiah's advent, founded on promises in the books of Moses, was generally received though the prophetic writings were not. The grammatical-historical bench documents that expectation. Barnes reasons that a people who received the five books of Moses would await the one they foretold; Robertson adds that Messias is a Hebrew word found in the New Testament only here and at John 1:41, that the Samaritans looked for a prophet like Moses (Deuteronomy 18:18), and that Samaria bred claimants, from Simon Magus (Acts 8:9) to the fanatical rising Pilate crushed. The Antiochene Chrysostom gives the same answer in the Catena: they held Moses, and Moses spoke of Christ — the scepter shall not depart from Judah... until Shiloh come (Genesis 49:10), and the Lord your God shall raise up a Prophet (Deuteronomy 18:15) — the passages from which, Ellicott adds, her countrymen expected "the Converter," "the Returner." Lightfoot alone troubles the harmony: the name Messias occurs nowhere in the Pentateuch, so whence did she learn it? His conjecture, from the rabbis' rules of synagogue reading: the Samaritans, like the Sadducees, barred the Prophets from public reading while privately using and believing them.

What she meant by it is variously weighed. Gill reads honest indecision: unsettled by what Christ had said of the place and manner of worship, she refers the whole to the Messiah's coming. Barnes agrees: Jesus had decided the worship question (v.20) for the Jews; unsatisfied, she looks to a Messiah expected soon, who will, in Clarke's phrase, "settle the great national question." Both hold that which is called Christ is the evangelist's customary gloss, for the woman knew no Greek. Ellicott hears something tenderer: bewildered by Father and Spirit, half-consciously feeling their power, she answers with a question; and her present tense — Messias cometh — trembles on the edge of a guess: can it be that he stands before her now (John 4:29)? Wesley hears simply joy, and desire of fuller instruction.

The Reformed Calvin takes her for a theologian in embryo: her one sentence confesses that the Law delivered no more than first principles, that the Messiah will be the interpreter of the Father and teacher of all the godly, and that beyond his doctrine it is unlawful to seek anything more perfect — would that the self-styled pillars of the church were as content with Christ's simple doctrine as this poor woman, instead of perfecting the gospel with the additions that built the religion of the Pope and of Mahomet. The fathers close in character: Augustine, for the Latin fathers, plays the three tongues — Messias in Hebrew, Christ in Greek, Unctus in Latin — and observes that she knew who could teach her without knowing Who was teaching her — a confirmation to discerning minds: after five carnal answers — the five senses, her five husbands — she at last names Christ. The Alexandrian Origen pauses to define Gospel itself, a discourse whose promised good gladdens the hearer, and remembers that Samaria produced its own false christ, one Dositheus; Cyril of Jerusalem sweeps the passage into his catechesis on the Holy Ghost, citing the true-worship saying (John 4:23-24) among the Savior's words concerning the Spirit.
26:
For the first time in this Gospel Jesus says the thing outright: I that speak unto thee am he. The grammatical-historical Robertson gives the Greek its full weight — "I am he, the one speaking to thee" — and observes that Jesus declares his Messiahship in language this plain only here and to the blind man (John 9:37). Ellicott hears the announcement happening in the act of speech: the solution of the problems she ties to Messiah's advent lies in the very words she has been hearing — and he points forward to the naked I am of John 8:24 and John 8:58. Gill marks the occasion simply: upon her mention of the Messiah, his coming and his work, Christ took the opportunity of making himself known.

Why here — to one obscure Samaritan — and not in Judea? The witnesses answer in chorus, each from his own side. Barnes: an open profession among the Jews would have excited envy and opposition; in Samaria nothing was to be apprehended, and since the woman, reluctant toward the prophet, professed willingness to listen to the Messiah, he declared himself "that by some means he might save her soul." The Wesleyan pair agree on the danger: Clarke cites Bishop Pearce — alone, and a Samaritan, she could not disturb his ministry before the time of his suffering came; our Lord never spoke so directly to his own countrymen, nor even to his disciples, till a little before his death. Wesley adds that Jews full of the Messiah's temporal kingdom would have taken up arms in his favor or accused him to the Roman governor; yet even among them he declared the thing in effect, under the titles Son of man and Son of God, while denying himself the name. The Antiochene Chrysostom, in the Catena, supplies the pastoral logic: had he said this at the beginning, it would have appeared vanity; now, having gradually awakened her to the thought of Christ, the disclosure is perfectly opportune. He was not equally open to the Jews who demanded If thou be the Christ, tell us plainly, because they asked not to learn but to do him injury — she spoke in the simplicity of her heart.

The Reformed Calvin turns the confession into a doctrine of Christ's teaching office. In owning himself Messiah he presents himself as her Teacher, in compliance with the expectation she had formed; probably, Calvin thinks, he proceeded to fuller instruction to satisfy her thirst. Such a proof of grace to this poor woman testifies that he never fails to discharge his office where he finds a ready disciple; while those who refuse to submit, or hope for a more perfect wisdom elsewhere, deserve to be driven about by innumerable enchantments and plunged at last into an abyss of errors.
