In the very name Messiah — the Anointed — he hears a seal set to ratify the doctrine of the gospel: Christ was anointed by the Father to bring us the message of salvation (Isaiah 61:1). Barnes closes on the evangelist's purpose: the scene shows Christ's knowledge of the heart and of the life — "he must therefore be divine" — supplies the very evidence John wrote his Gospel to record, models the wisdom that turns conversation toward the soul, and defines religion itself: not external form, but a pure, spiritual, ever-bubbling fountain, the worship of a holy God from an humble heart.
27:
It takes Lightfoot's rabbinic notebook to measure the shock. "Do not multiply discourse with a woman," ran the wise men's maxim — Berurea, the learned wife of Rabbi Meir, flung it at Rabbi Jose the Galilean for asking What way must we go to Lydda? when Which way to Lydda? would have served — and "a woman ought not to be wise above her distaff." The grammatical-historical pair sharpen the object of wonder from the Greek: it is not the woman but a woman — they marveled that their rabbi talked publicly with any woman, under precepts by which, Ellicott says, the words of the Law should be burnt rather than taught to a woman; Robertson's imperfect tense keeps them staring, feeling that Jesus was acting beneath a rabbi's dignity. Wesley and Clarke widen the marvel — a woman, and of that nation peculiarly hateful to the Jews; Clarke notes the eastern custom, alive in his own day, that a man does not speak even to his own wife in the street. The Reformed Calvin reduces it to two causes — offense at the woman's mean condition, or the notion that a Jew was polluted by Samaritan conversation — both born of devout reverence for their Master, and both wrong: why did they not rather look at themselves, men of no note, almost the offscourings of the people, raised to the highest rank of honor?

The silence weighs as much as the marveling. Chrysostom: for all their wonder they asked no question, so great was their awe and veneration, so careful were they to keep the rank of disciples — on matters concerning themselves they questioned him freely, but not here. Wesley parses the two unasked questions: none said to the woman, What seekest thou?, nor to Christ, Why talkest thou with her? Robertson hears the aged John still remembering their amazement and their unwillingness to reflect upon Jesus; Barnes draws the plain lesson — be confident that Jesus is right even when we cannot understand him; and Calvin makes it a standing rule: when anything in the works or words of God offends our feelings, murmur nothing, but wait in modest silence until what is hidden be revealed from heaven.

Whether the marveling itself was innocent is the one quarrel. Augustine acquits them wholly: he who came to seek that which was lost was seeking the lost one; they marveled at his goodness and suspected no evil. The Puritan Matthew Henry, whose exposition here sweeps on across the whole Samaritan story, is less sure: perhaps there was something amiss in their marveling, something like the Pharisees' offense at his eating with publicans and sinners; they thought their Master should be as shy of Samaritans as other Jews were, forgetting what despicable men they themselves had been when he called them. Yet even Henry praises the acquiescence: they knew it was for some good reason, of which he was not bound to give them an account, and whatever they thought, they said nothing — if thou hast thought evil, lay thine hand upon thy mouth (Proverbs 30:32). Gill, matter-of-fact, times the entrance: the disciples, back from buying food in the city, came up just as he was saying I that speak unto thee am he — the interruption, Henry observes, falling only after the discourse was brought to a head: the daughters of Jerusalem shall not stir up nor awake love till he please.
28:
An abandoned waterpot detains every school, and the reasons given for it map the whole cloud. Most read haste. The Reformed Calvin calls the forgotten pitcher the evangelist's way of expressing the ardor of her zeal, and turns it into a definition: it is the nature of faith, once we are partakers of eternal life, to wish others to share with us — I believed, and therefore I will speak (Psalm 116:10); the marvel is that so small a spark of faith kindled such promptitude, which shames the sluggishness of those far advanced in Christ's school. To the objection that an ignorant woman overstepped her limits he answers that she assumed no teacher's office, but merely did the office of a trumpet or a bell to invite men to Christ. Barnes sees excitement and agitation; Clarke, a mind so penetrated that she forgot her errand; Wesley, a "forgetting of smaller things"; Gill, either a pot left for Christ and his disciples to use or, rather, sheer forgetfulness — though he passes on the Ethiopic version's oddity, "she left her disputation." Robertson supplies the grammar: an ingressive aorist, "in her excitement and embarrassment," adding drily that the vessel was too large for speed anyhow (John 2:6).

The Antiochene Chrysostom preaches the zeal at full length: without fervor and uproused zeal none of the promised blessings can be obtained — I am come to send fire upon the earth (Luke 12:49; compare Matthew 10:38) — and such a disciple was this woman, so kindled by his words that she left the very purpose for which she came. The apostles, when called, left their nets; she, of her own accord and without any command, leaves her waterpot and, winged by joy, performs the office of an evangelist — calling not one or two, as Andrew and Philip did, but a whole city.

Lightfoot, against the current, thinks it simple courtesy: it was kindly done to leave the waterpot behind, that Jesus and his disciples might have wherewithal to drink. The Puritan Matthew Henry serenely holds all the readings at once: she left it in kindness to Christ, like Rebecca's civility to Abraham's servant (Genesis 24:18; see Matthew 10:42); she left it for haste, since those who publish the name of Christ must not encumber themselves with anything that would retard them; and she left it careless of it, with that holy contempt of this world which marks those newly acquainted with the things of God. Augustine, for the Latin fathers, allegorizes: the waterpot signifies the love of this world, concupiscence, by which men draw pleasure from the dark depth of which the well is the image; leaving it, she shows she has parted with worldly desires — let those who would preach the gospel first leave their waterpots at the well. Origen matches him: she gave up low bodily cares for the sake of benefiting others, and — having had five husbands and then a sixth who was no husband — she gives him up for a seventh and, leaving her waterpot, is converted to chastity. Ellicott stands apart with a historian's eye: the waterpot left behind was a pledge of her return — and, for us, a quiet mark of the presence of the eyewitness who has related the incidents.
29:
Come, see — not, Come, believe. The Antiochene Chrysostom fastens on the tact of the invitation: she did not wish them to trust her report alone, but to come and judge for themselves — seeing is an easier matter than believing; well she knew that if they only tasted of that well, they would feel as she did. Shame does not detain her: the soul once kindled by the divine flame regards neither glory nor shame nor any earthly thing, only the flame that consumes it. Alcuin notes the same tact in her titles: she comes to the preaching of Christ only by degrees, first calling him a man, lest her hearers grow angry and refuse to come; and the question that follows — is not this the Christ? — neither openly preaches him nor wholly omits him, and so, says Chrysostom, wakened their attention. Gill glosses the invitation itself: come, see an extraordinary man — and "come," not "go," for she means to return with them: those who have tasted that the Lord is gracious desire more grace and more communion. The grammatical-historical men confirm the tact from the particle: Robertson observes that with a woman's intuition she asks in the hesitant form that avoids taking sides and piques curiosity; Ellicott would translate "is this the Christ?" — a question not framed to expect yes: she states the fact of his knowledge and leaves them to their own judgment, having climbed the same steps herself — prophet (John 4:19), the thought of Messiah (John 4:25), his own declaration (John 4:26). Barnes hears modesty in so important a case; Calvin, a woman unqualified for high mysteries wisely offering the sign as a "lower preparation," content if they would seek of their own accord what she had found — they would find more than she promised; Wesley, no doubt in herself, only an incitement to inquiry.

Her evidence — he told me all things that ever I did — raises the verse's one real quarrel. Robertson notes that there is no ever in the Greek, and calls the "all" the exaggeration of a guilty conscience. Calvin asks the blunter form of the question — why does she tell a lie? — and acquits her: Christ had set before her, in a few words, the sins of her whole life, the evangelist recording only the summary; kindled by holy zeal, she does not spare her own reputation to magnify the name of Christ. Wesley mediates: our Lord had told her but a few things, but his word awakened her conscience, which soon told her all the rest; and Barnes allows that he perhaps told her much more than is recorded.

Two witnesses widen the frame. Lightfoot brings the rabbinic test of the Messiah's "scent" (Isaiah 11:3): Ben Coziba, who claimed the title, was killed when he could not "smell and judge" — and this woman argued precisely so, that one who had smelt out her clandestine deeds must be the Messiah; Clarke tells the same story of Barchochab, put to death when he could not tell the secrets of hearts (Isaiah 11:2-3). And Cyril of Jerusalem turns it into a preacher's antithesis: the chief priests knew him not, and the devils confessed him; the chief priests knew him not, and a woman of Samaria proclaimed him — Come, see a man which told me all things that ever I did.
30:
Two Greek tenses carry the whole movement of this verse, and the grammatical-historical school will not let the English blur them. Robertson parses both: they went out is an effective aorist — out of the city at once, in a rush — while and came unto him is really an imperfect, "were coming," graphically picturing the long procession as it approached Jesus. Ellicott makes the same correction, and he and Clarke independently draw the harmonistic consequence: the Samaritans were still on the way while the whole discourse on food and harvest (John 4:31-38) passed between Jesus and his disciples, the people of Sychar only arriving at v.40. Gill adds a version's precision: the Syriac expressly reads "the men" — the men of the city to whom the woman had spoken — and the inhabitants of Sychar left their business to come out to him. Barnes gives the motive in a line: they went to Jesus, to hear and to examine for themselves; and the Wesleyan Clarke marvels at the cause — "such effect had the simple testimony of the woman on their minds."

Ellicott widens the lens: her alacrity in going to tell the men of the place, and their desire at once to see him for themselves, disclose both the general expectation of the Messiah abroad in that generation and the peculiarly receptive spirit of the Samaritans — many of whom, he recalls, were convinced by her statement alone (John 4:39-40).

The Puritan Matthew Henry lingers longest over the procession. It might seem very improbable, he grants, that a woman of so small a figure and so ill a character should have the honor of the first discovery of the Messiah among the Samaritans; but it pleased God to incline their hearts to heed her report and not slight it as an idle tale — time was when lepers were the first to bring tidings of a great deliverance to Samaria (2 Kings 7:3). He marks, too, the direction of the movement: they did not send for Christ into the city, but in token of their respect and the earnestness of their desire they went out to him — those that would know Christ must meet him where he records his name. And he ties the verse to what follows: these very crowds coming over the fields, many of them probably already in view, are what Christ will presently bid his disciples look upon as fields already white unto harvest.
31:
Between the woman's departure and the town's arrival there falls a meal, and the Alexandrian Origen fixes the timing: the disciples judge this the convenient hour for dining precisely because it lies after her going into the city and before the coming of the Samaritans, so that they sit at meat by themselves — that, he says, is what in the mean while means. Gill and Wesley say the same in a clause apiece — while she was telling the city; before the people came — and Robertson observes that the Greek phrase is elliptical, "in the between," with "time" understood. Augustine sets the scene: the disciples had gone to buy food and had returned, and offered Christ some. Ellicott remembers what they had left behind: a Master weary by the well's side (John 4:6); and he pauses over their address, for the word is Rabbi, as at John 1:38, John 1:49, and John 3:2 — less ambiguous than the English "Master," and he would have it restored here as at John 9:2 and John 11:28.

The entreaty itself the witnesses read as affection. The Antiochene Chrysostom: they all ask him at once — him, so fatigued with the journey and the heat — and this is not impatience in them, but simple love and tenderness toward their Master. The grammatical-historical Robertson finds the same tenderness lodged in the tense: prayed him is imperfect, "they kept beseeching him," the verb bearing its late sense of beseech rather than question (as again at v.40 and v.47); and he adds the fine psychological note that their concern for the comfort of Jesus overcame their surprise about the woman.

Origen alone goes beneath the surface of the request. The disciples had procured humble food among the people of the country and beseech him to eat, fearing perhaps lest the Word of God, deprived of his own proper nourishment, should fail within them; they would feed him that, confirmed and strengthened, he may abide with his nourishers. For souls require food as truly as bodies do, and differ as bodies differ in capacity and in need — the newborn require the milk of the word, the grown-up solid meat — a thought that quietly prepares his reading of the answer Jesus is about to give. No other witness follows the allegory, but it grows from the same soil as Chrysostom's plainer observation: everyone at this well, in his own idiom, is anxious that someone else should eat.
32:
Why does a famished, road-weary man refuse his dinner? The Reformed Calvin will not let the paradox pass unexamined. It is wonderful, he says, that when fatigued and hungry Christ declines to eat; but the lesson is not asceticism — had he meant to teach bare refusal of food, he would have done so always. Rather, anxiety about the business in hand so absorbs his whole mind that despising food costs him nothing; he shows what must be done first and what afterwards — that the kingdom of God is to be preferred to all the comforts of the body. God kindly permits us to care for the body so far as necessity requires; but we must not cleave so firmly to our fixed hours as to be unable to forgo a meal when God, as it were, fixes the present hour. Christ, with an opportunity in his hands that might pass away, embraces it with open arms — and indeed, Calvin adds, when the woman had left her pitcher and run to call the town, it would have been unreasonable for Christ to display less zeal than she. His summary is a maxim: we must not lose the causes of life for the sake of life itself. Even the figure pleases him — a metaphor of eating drawn seasonably from the business of the hour.

The fathers tell us what the hidden meat is. The Antiochene Chrysostom: the salvation of men he calls his food, showing the greatness of his desire that we should be saved — as food is an object of desire to us, so was the salvation of men to him; and he chose the figurative expression deliberately, since what costs the hearer some trouble to comprehend acquires the greater importance when it is understood. Theophylact adds the occasion: the Lord says it knowing that the woman is even now bringing the whole town out to him; and that ye know not of means either that they knew not that he calls the salvation of men his food, or that they knew not that the Samaritans were about to believe and be saved. The Alexandrian Origen turns the saying into a rule for all teachers: one who is set over the weak, who cannot yet behold what the stronger behold, may always say I have meat to eat that ye know not of.

The grammatical-historical witnesses weigh the words. Ellicott puts the emphasis on the opposed pronouns — I have food; ye know it not — and notes that the word, better rendered food, is the very one that will bear the weight of John 6:27 and John 6:55. Robertson traces the noun from the act of eating (Romans 14:17) to the thing eaten, like the word of the next verse (v.34): "spiritual food Jesus had." Gill contributes only a textual crumb — the Persic and Ethiopic versions supply the name, "Jesus said" — while the Wesleyan Clarke states the method the verse exhibits: our Lord seizes every opportunity to raise the minds of his hearers from earthly to heavenly things through the medium of earthly matters, nor does he ever force the figure; rightly understood, earthly substances are the types, representatives, and shadows of heavenly things.
33:
Augustine catches the symmetry at once: what wonder that the woman did not understand about the water, when — behold — the disciples do not understand about the meat. The dullness is general, and the grammatical-historical commentators are content to fix its grammar. The question is asked with the particle that expects the answer no — "surely no one hath brought him anything to eat?"; "during our absence, they mean," Robertson adds, noting the same infinitive construction as the request for a drink that began the scene (John 4:7). Ellicott completes the unspoken thought: the only person who had been with him was this Samaritan woman — surely she has not! They understand his words in the ordinary sense, and he must proceed, in the next verse (v.34), to explain their real meaning. Barnes files the verse without ceremony among "the many instances in which the disciples were slow to understand the Saviour," and Gill notes the manner of it: they said it privately, one to another, among themselves though in his hearing — and he knew what they said, as his answer will show.

The Wesleyan Clarke alone credits their guess with some theology. "Hath any man brought him aught to eat?" might mean: has he got food in some preternatural way? For they could not help remembering Elijah fed by ravens at the brook Cherith (1 Kings 17:4-6) and by the ministry of an angel (1 Kings 19:5-8), and their Lord's own preternatural repast in the wilderness after his victory over Satan (Matthew 4:11).

The fathers, whose remarks stand under v.31 in the Catena, hear two further things in the whispered question. The Antiochene Chrysostom hears reverence, the same that had sealed their lips before (v.27): they show, as usual, the honor in which they hold their Master by talking among themselves and not presuming to question him. Theophylact draws from it a small doctrine of ministry: the very form of the question shows that our Lord was accustomed to receive food from others when it was offered — not that he who gives food to all flesh needed any assistance, but he received it that the givers might obtain their reward, that poverty might thenceforth not blush, and that to be supported by others might no longer be esteemed a disgrace; it is proper and even necessary, he concludes, that teachers should depend on others for their food, so that, freed from all other cares, they may attend the more to the ministry of the word.
34:
The Wesleyan John Wesley compresses the verse into a single definition: my meat is "that which satisfies the strongest appetite of my soul." The grammatical-historical men unfold the grammar of that appetite. Ellicott would translate my food, the same word the disciples had gone to buy (John 4:8), and reads the two verbs as the end Christ ever kept in view — constantly doing the Father's will, then at last completing His work — noting that the manuscripts differ over the tenses (John 17:4). Robertson finds in the Greek construction the Messianic consciousness of Jesus, clear and steady (John 5:30; John 6:38) — he never doubted that the Father sent him — and the verb rendered finish runs forward through John 5:36 and the Intercessory Prayer to the cry from the cross, Tetelestai, "It is finished." He will carry through the Father's programme (John 3:16); and his food just now, Robertson adds, had been winning the woman to God.

Barnes ties the saying to the riddle of v.32 which it explains: Jesus came to the well weary, thirsty, and probably hungry; an opportunity of doing good presented itself, and he forgot fatigue and hunger in the joy of seeking to save a soul. The mind may be so absorbed in doing the will of God as to rise above fatigue, hardship, and want — he quotes Job, "I have esteemed the words of his mouth more than my necessary food" (Job 23:12) — and the main business of life, he presses, is not the supply of temporal necessities but to do the will of God.

The Reformed Calvin refines the psychology: Christ means not merely that he esteems the Father's will highly, but that there is nothing in which he takes greater delight — as David, magnifying the Law, will not stop at saying that he values it (Psalms 19:10). Two lessons follow. If we would follow Christ, our service must be so cheerful that the labour is never oppressive; and every man ought to consider his own calling, lest he count as done to God what he rashly undertook at his own suggestion — Christ's known office being to advance the kingdom of God, restore lost souls, and spread the light of the Gospel. From this Calvin draws no ordinary consolation: the Christ who took his highest delight in procuring men's salvation is actuated by similar feelings towards us now. Gill pauses only to fix the audience — the them are his disciples.

Clarke turns the verse toward the pulpit: here is a lesson of zeal and earnestness to the apostles and all their successors in the ministry — let the salvation of souls lie nearer your heart than life itself; let eating and drinking, labour and rest, reading, study, and prayer all be directed to this one work; souls are dropping by thousands into hell — "O pluck the brands out of the burning!" Ellicott ends more quietly, with analogies from within every man's experience of a food others know not of: the command of duty, the cheering power of hope, the stimulus of success supply weak and weary nerves with the vigour of a new life — under them the soldier forgets his wounds, the martyr smiles at the flame, the worn-out traveller plods onward at the thought of home (compare Matthew 4:4).
35:
Is yet four months a note of the calendar or a sower's proverb? The question divides the grammatical-historical school against itself. Barnes takes it for a proverb — "ye say," that is, men say — naming the common interval from sowing to harvest. Ellicott answers flatly that there is no evidence of a proverb, and the form of the sentence is against it: the words are a note of time, placing the conversation about the middle of Tebeth (our January), since harvest legally opened on the 16th of Nisan (Leviticus 23:10; Deuteronomy 16:9). Gill agrees — eight months spent since the last passover, four remaining to the next — and Clarke works the computation through the rabbinic table of the seasons, calling the fixing of this epoch of considerable importance, though he finally concludes the Lord meant no more than an illustration. Lightfoot, from the same Jewish calendar, adds the sharpest observation: the barley whose harvest lay four months off was not yet even committed to the ground — and yet the Saviour says the fields are white already. Robertson referees: it is not possible to tell, though the "yet" and the actual length of the growing season argue against the proverb; if literal, Jesus spoke in December.

On the second clause the schools are of one mind: the whitening fields are the Samaritans. Theophylact sets the contrast — you expect a material harvest, but a spiritual harvest is at hand — and the Antiochene Chrysostom explains why the Lord teaches from the objects around them: the expectant crowd now approaching is the white field, for as corn grown white is ready for the harvest, so were these ready for salvation; the pictured speech gives his words vividness and sinks deeper into memory. Wesley: the Samaritans, ripe for the Gospel, covered the ground round about them. Clarke sees them coming in troops, guided by the woman — and hazards the conjecture that their white raiment helped the image. Augustine catches the urgency — intent on beginning the work, Christ hastened to send labourers — and the Latin and Greek fathers run on into the sayings that follow: the Prophets sowed, the Apostles reaped (Chrysostom); Augustine sees one harvest gathered when thousands laid their possessions at the Apostles' feet, and another at the end of the world, reaped by angels.

The Alexandrian Origen refuses to leave the four months literal while the fields are allegorical: the same principle of interpretation must govern both. For him the four months are the four elements — our natural life — and the harvest is the end of the world; the disciples defer the knowledge of truth, and this the Lord condemns: lift up your eyes now. The fields are white whenever the Word lights up the fields of Scripture; all sensible things are whitening fields, if only reason be at hand to interpret them. The Reformed Calvin draws the practical spring: ripe corn cannot bear delay, or the grain falls to the ground and is lost; and the saying shames us, who carefully reckon up months and days for the earthly harvest but are astonishingly drowsy about the heavenly wheat (Matthew 16:3). Barnes turns it to encouragement: the gospel is fitted to make immediate impression — scarce was the seed sown at Sychar before it produced an abundant harvest (Matthew 9:37) — and a preacher pleading weariness, hunger, or impropriety would have lost it all.
36:
One small Greek adverb sets the tone. Clarke and Robertson both move already forward out of v.35 to open this verse — already the reaper receiveth wages — Clarke on the authority of some excellent manuscripts and versions. The spiritual harvester, Robertson explains, need not wait four months: Jesus is reaping a harvest right now, in the conversion of this woman; Amos's promise that the ploughman shall overtake the reaper (Amos 9:13) has happened here, with the joy of the harvest time (Isaiah 9:3) — sower and reapers rejoicing simultaneously, "together."

Who, then, is reaping? The main line — Ellicott, Robertson, the Wesleyan Wesley — makes Christ the Sower, whose words have been the seed in the woman's heart, and the disciples the reapers who gather the Samaritans in; Wesley adds that their rejoicing together is in heaven. Clarke reads it otherwise: sower and reaper are here one and the same person, the heavenly sower Jesus Christ, who had not time to leave the field he had sown before it was full time to gather in the harvest — seed-time and harvest on the same day. The Reformed Gill widens the reapers to gospel ministers at large, and works the figure hard: as the sickle cuts down the ripe corn and gathers it into sheaves, so the ministry lays low the loftiness of man, strips him of dependence on his own righteousness, and gathers converts into the churches; the wages are both a comfortable maintenance in providence and the delight of being made useful. Barnes agrees the labourer in this harvest will not go unrewarded (Daniel 12:3; Matthew 19:28), and turns the joint rejoicing into a picture of the united work: Paul may plant and Apollos water while God gives the increase (1 Corinthians 3:5-9), and the Sunday-school teacher who sows in early life shall rejoice with the minister who gathers the harvest. Ellicott defines the wages most simply: the joy — the greatest the heart can know — of gathering others into eternal life.

Calvin will not let the word wages unsettle his doctrine. The promised reward is meant to hearten ministers, not to ground merit: which of us, come to a reckoning, would not be found worthier of punishment for slothfulness than of reward for diligence? That God finds anything in our works to crown is because he bestowed it by his Spirit, the earnest of adoption (Ephesians 1:14), and he accepts imperfect works without regard to merit — so the passage confirms justification by faith rather than overturning it. Calvin then reads sowing and reaping as Law and Gospel: the doctrine of the Prophets left the seed, as it were, in the blade, while the Gospel brings men to proper maturity — the heir who differs nothing from a servant until the appointed time (Galatians 4:1-2); and if this harvest ripens in Samaria, it is because some small portion of prophetic seed had been carried thither, a hidden seed of piety — which is why one word about Christ finds them eager for a Redeemer. He expressly refuses to extend the figure to the degenerate seed of the philosophers. Last, Cyril of Jerusalem, catechizing on life eternal, cites the verse to prove that eternal life is gained by the preaching of the Gospel, as elsewhere by faith.
37:
Every school knows this proverb from somewhere. The grammatical-historical Barnes finds it in the Greek writers (he credits Grotius for the instances) and in similar sayings among the Jews (Isaiah 65:21-22; Leviticus 26:16; Micah 6:15). Clarke quotes the Greek proverb outright, the Latin equivalent — "you have beat the bush, and another has found the hare" — and Virgil's famous Sic vos non vobis verses, where birds, sheep, bees, and oxen all labour, but not for themselves. Ellicott demurs: the reference is probably rather to the Old Testament scriptures, for those who heard it would think of passages like Deuteronomy 6:11. Robertson maps the biblical uses on both sides: sowing that another reaps may be the punishment of sin (Deuteronomy 28:30), and reaping where one has not sown, the gift of grace (Joshua 24:13).

What the Lord does with the proverb is the finer point. In the world's mouth, Ellicott says, the saying expresses something of the bitterness of human disappointment which in darker moments all men have felt — hopes and plans sown that never sprang above the surface, or were reaped in their results by other men. But herein, in the relation of gospel sower and reaper, the saying is "true" in John's deeper sense of that word (John 1:9) — ideally true, realised: the sower too has food in the will of Him that sent him, and his satisfaction, hard for the world to understand, has been the strength of the best lives and noblest deeds of humanity. Robertson strikes the same balance: it is sad when the sower misses the joy of reaping and has only the sowing in tears (Job 31:8; Psalm 126:5); it is the Master's prerogative to reap (Matthew 25:26), but Jesus here lets the disciples share his joy.

Who sows and who reaps is put most compactly by the Wesleyan Wesley: one soweth — the prophets and Christ; another reapeth — the apostles and succeeding ministers. Clarke agrees: the patriarchs and prophets announced the Messiah and excited the expectation of the people, but were gathered to their fathers before they could see the fruit of their labour. The Reformed Calvin draws two fences around the saying. First, the prophets are companions of the apostles' joy only in this sense, that while they lived they rejoiced over fruit they were not permitted to gather; it cannot be inferred that the prophets are now witnesses of what goes on in the Church — he compares Peter's word that they ministered not to themselves (1 Peter 1:12). Second, the proverb becomes a rule for ministers: labours go into a common stock, that there be no wicked envy between those first sent and those who gather the ripe fruit after them. Gill notes simply that the proverbial expression, so much in use, is here verified, and may be applied to different persons and cases. And Clarke preserves Bishop Pearce's remarkable turn on the clause that follows: Jesus "sent them away" to the city (John 4:8) not merely to buy meat, but chiefly that a harvest sown in their absence might be waiting for them to reap on their return.
38:
Who are the other men that laboured? The grammatical-historical witnesses return different censuses. Barnes lists four: the prophets, who long laboured to prepare the way of the Messiah; the teachers among the Jews, who read and explained the law; John the Baptist; and the Saviour himself — who, Barnes observes, saw comparatively little fruit of his own ministry, confining his labours to Judea and sowing seed which chiefly sprang up after his death. Robertson's roll is similar — Jesus, the Baptist, the prophets — but for Sychar itself he is precise: the disciples had done no sowing there at all; only Jesus and the woman had laboured, and the emphatic "I sent you" sets the reapers in deliberate contrast with them. Ellicott narrows furthest: in the immediate application the "others" is Christ Himself, who had been sowing during their absence — and it may be the woman, who sowed by her testimony — the plural being chosen either in contrast with the plural "ye," or as pointing to the general truth while the reference is to Christ only. For He is ever the Sower, Ellicott says; all others are more or less fully reapers, and only in the degree in which they really reap do they become sowers too — as, in another register, we all inherit from the past, and the child of to-day knows more than the philosopher of early history. The Reformed Gill reads "I sent you to reap" simply of the disciples' commission: to preach the Gospel and gather in souls by their ministry.

Barnes then draws the working lessons that have made this a ministers' text. The man crowned with eminent success has no cause of boasting over others, any more than the reaper of a field may boast over the man who sowed it: the labour of both is equally necessary, and both would be useless did not God give the increase (1 Corinthians 3:6). Nor should the sower be discouraged when no harvest immediately springs up: we sow in the morning and withhold not the hand in the evening, not knowing which shall prosper (Ecclesiastes 11:4), and go forth weeping with precious seed, knowing we shall come again rejoicing, bearing sheaves (Psalms 126:6). And since every part of the work is needful, to bear any part in it, however humble, is matter for rejoicing (1 Corinthians 12:21-24). Robertson closes the arc historically: "ye are entered into their labour" means entered into the fruit and blessed results of their toil — and this, he notes, is always true, as was seen when Philip preached in Samaria and the apostles came down to that harvest (Acts 8:5-7; Acts 8:14).
39:
Clarke crowns the woman with a title the rest only imply: she was "the first apostle of Christ in Samaria"; and the twice-repeated he told me all that ever I did (v.29) intimates, he thinks, a more particular conversation at the well than the Evangelist has related. The Antiochene Chrysostom admires the townsmen's logic: they considered that the woman would never of her own accord have conceived such admiration for one who had reproved her offences, unless he were really some great and wonderful person — and so, relying solely on her testimony, without any other evidence, they went out to Christ. It is now, he says, as it were harvest time, the corn gathered, a whole floor soon covered with sheaves. Ellicott states the contrast that governs the scene: the Jews refused the witness of a great prophet; these accept the witness of a woman, their minds prepared by the general expectation of Messiah and met by a sign they do not question — Jesus had revealed to her the whole past of her life. Robertson praises the witness herself: she bore it clearly and with discretion, frankly confessing Christ's rebuke, telling enough to bring her neighbours — she had her share in this harvest — and how timid and cowardly, he adds, we often are today in giving our testimony to our neighbour. Barnes generalises: there is seldom an instance of so remarkable a success — from a single conversation, in unpromising circumstances, with an unlikely individual, many converted and a city stirred — so we should never despair of doing good, and should seize every opportunity to speak with sinners about their souls.

But what sort of believing was this? The Reformed Calvin says the word is here used inaccurately: the woman's statement induced them to acknowledge Christ as a Prophet — a commencement of faith, minds prepared to receive the doctrine; and such docility receives the honourable name of faith, to teach us how highly God esteems reverence for his word. The Alexandrian Origen falls on the same side: the woman's report was the ground of their belief, the enlightening power of the Word itself not yet visible to them — they were not yet ready, he says, for the third day. Augustine makes the sequence a figure of all conversion: they knew Christ first by report of another, afterwards by his own presence, "which is still the case of those that are without the fold"; Christ is announced by some charitable Christian — by the report of the woman, that is, the Church — men come, believe, and when he has stayed with them (his two days, Augustine ventures, the two precepts of charity), their belief stands the stronger. Origen adds his customary allegory: the command not to enter a city of the Samaritans forbade the doctrines of those who believe the Scriptures but interpret them heretically; when men give up their own doctrines — go out of the city — and come to Jesus, it is lawful to stay with them. Gill anchors the geography: the city is Sychar, a city of Samaria.
40:
Nothing is worse than envy and malice, nothing more mischievous than vainglory — so the Antiochene Chrysostom opens, for he cannot read this verse except against the Jews. They excelled the Samaritans in knowledge and had been always familiar with the Prophets; yet when they had beheld his wonders they not only did not detain him, but used every means to cast him out of their land — though his very coming had been for their sake — while the Samaritans, who had seen no sign and bore hostile feelings toward the Jews, came forth beseeching Christ to tarry with them. Was it not fitting, he asks, that he should receive those who asked and besought him, rather than wait upon those who plotted against and repulsed him? So he both accepted them and tarried — yet only two days, though they desired to keep him among them continually; and the Jews, even with signs, remained uncorrected, while these, without signs, manifested great faith, and glory in that very thing.

The Wesleyan Clarke fastens on what is absent: no miracle is recorded at Sychar, and none, he argues, was necessary — a simple-hearted, teachable people, they credited him on the evidence of his own eternal truth. From this he answers a standing question of his own day: why are not miracles wrought now? Miracles were for the establishment of the doctrines of Christianity where they were first preached; where the doctrine is credited, no miracle is necessary.

The grammatical-historical pair mark the manner and the marvel. Ellicott: the next step in their faith is to go to him and ask him to remain, that they too may learn of him — and he, a Jew, accepts the hospitality of Samaria and abides with them two days. Robertson reads two days as the accusative of extent of time — they wanted to cultivate the acquaintance of Jesus — and calls the stay a continuous revival, a most unexpected experience when one recalls the feeling between Jews and Samaritans (v.9): the reaping went on gloriously. Gill supplies the frame: the Ethiopic version reads "all the Samaritans"; they came to him at Jacob's well upon the woman's solicitations, and after they had conversed with him and heard him themselves, they were taken with his divine discourses and thoroughly persuaded that he was the Messiah.
41:
A veil, says Ellicott, is left upon those two days, as upon so many days in the life of Christ; and his comment is mostly a reverent lifting of its edge. We know how much was said at the well in a few minutes, and that many believed on him in a few hours: what questions they must have asked, what truths he must have taught, in a sojourn of two days! Salvation was of the Jews, and this mixed and despised people were from Babylon and Cuthah, from Ava, Hamath, and Sepharvaim; but the Fatherhood of God and the brotherhood of man is a truth for every heart of man, and it must, he says, have burned in theirs. The result the Evangelist does record: many more believed, and those who before believed on testimony passed to the higher faith of personal conviction. His grammatical-historical colleague Robertson weighs the comparative: "many more" is literally "more by much" than the "many" of v.39 — Jesus was reaping more rapidly than the woman had — and the verb, he insists, means that they really believed.

The Reformed voices finish the account. Gill notes that the Vulgate Latin and all the Oriental versions supply the object — "believed on him" — and that this larger multitude believed when Christ was come into the city and had preached to its inhabitants in general; they believed in him as the Messiah, professed him, and became his followers. Calvin draws the lesson of the stay itself: what followed proved Christ's compliance with the Samaritans' wish highly proper, for we see how much fruit was reaped from the two days granted to their request. By this example, he writes, we are taught never to refrain from working when we have it in our power to advance the kingdom of God; and if we are afraid that our readiness in complying may be liable to unfavourable reports, or may often prove useless, let us ask from Christ the Spirit of counsel to direct us. And he marks the change in the word since v.39: "believed" is now used in a different sense — no longer minds merely prepared for faith, but an actual and proper faith.
42:
Their parting word to the woman becomes, in Robertson's hands, a small study in the vocabulary of faith. "Not because of thy speaking" is literally "no longer because of thy talk" — lalia, talk, one's mode of speech, a word Jesus elsewhere uses of his own speech (John 8:43) — good and effective as her talk had been; and "we have heard" is a perfect tense, their abiding experience. The Reformed Calvin handles the same word more roughly: the Samaritans appear to boast that they now have a stronger foundation than a woman's tongue, "which is, for the most part, light and trivial." But his real interest is in what their faith now rests on: it has been drawn from the word of God itself, so that they can boast of having the Son of God as their Teacher, on whose authority alone faith can safely rely. True, Christ is not now visibly present to speak with us mouth to mouth; but by whomsoever we hear him, faith can rest on none but himself — the first thing necessary is to know that it is Christ who speaks by his ministers, and the next, to give him the honour due; for speech from the mouth of a mortal man may fill the ears, but will never confirm the soul in the calm confidence of salvation.

Ellicott's textual note strips the confession to its keenest form: the "him" of "heard him ourselves" is no part of the original text — "we have ourselves heard" — and probably "the Christ" is not either, so that the clause runs, "and know that this is truly the Saviour of the world." The result of their hearing is that they know: St. John's characteristic stress on the development of faith, which we shall meet again in the courtier. Gill meanwhile watches the woman: she kept hearing Christ, attending on him and conversing with him, for having tasted of his grace she could not leave him.

The great phrase of the confession gets its fullest defence from Robertson. Against Bernard's "amazing statement" that the title Saviour is not proven for Messiah in the first century, he stacks Deissmann's inscriptions — Julius Caesar, Augustus, Vespasian, Hadrian, and other emperors were hailed in the Hellenistic East as "saviour of the world" — and the New Testament's own first-century usage (Luke 2:11; Acts 5:31; 1 John 4:14). And to Sanday's suggestion that the epithet may be merely synonymous with Messiah, he answers: why "merely"? Jesus had told the woman that salvation is of the Jews (v.22) and that he was Messiah (v.26); in the two days he clearly taught the Samaritans the same, and these villagers, taking a Jew for their Saviour, rise of themselves to the conception of the whole world. The Wesleyan Clarke says it plainly: the Saviour not of the Jews only, but of the Samaritans and of the whole Gentile world. And Calvin ends where Paul does: Christ testified that the salvation he brought was common to the whole world, that they — not called as lawful heirs, as the Jews were — might understand that it belonged to them also; he came to admit them into the family of God, preaching peace to them that were afar off (Ephesians 2:17).
43:
Two quiet days in Samaria end here, and Gill pauses to note their fruit before moving on: the stay at Sychar, "conversing with, and discoursing to the Samaritans," was the means of the conversion of many. Ellicott and Robertson add the small grammatical anchor — literally the two days, the very ones named in v.40, and the journey now resumed is the one interrupted at the well, begun back in v.3.

But the verse carries a famous puzzle in its skirts: Jesus goes into Galilee — and the next breath explains it by the proverb that a prophet has no honour in his own country. The Latin Augustine states the difficulty candidly: would he not have testified more to the truth of the saying by staying in Samaria? His answer is a grim symmetry — Christ stayed two days in Samaria and the Samaritans believed; he stayed as long in Galilee, and the Galileans did not believe. The Antiochene Chrysostom relieves the tension geographically: the Lord's own country is Capernaum, the city that would be exalted to heaven and brought down to hell (Matthew 11:23), and it is precisely there he does not go, choosing Cana instead; Theophylact reads the clause as explaining why Jesus was not always in Galilee — because of the little honour he received there.

The moderns mostly narrow the "country" to Nazareth. Wesley is concise: into the country of Galilee, "but not to Nazareth. It was at that town only that he had no honour." Barnes agrees — evidently not Nazareth, but probably straight to Cana (John 4:46); Clarke reports Bishop Pearce's conjecture that words have dropped from the verse — "went into Galilee, but not to Nazareth, for Jesus himself had declared" — observing that in every Synoptic use of the saying (Matthew 13:57; Luke 4:24) Nazareth alone is meant, while Galilee at large was where he lived, wrought most of his miracles, and made most converts. The Puritan Matthew Henry turns the proverb over pastorally: prophets ought to have honour, yet it is most often denied them at home — Joseph hated by his brethren, David disdained by his brother (1 Samuel 17:28), Jeremiah maligned by Anathoth (Jeremiah 11:21), Christ slighted by his own kinsmen (John 7:5). Pride and envy, he says, make men scorn to be instructed by those who were once their schoolfellows; and it is just with God to deny his gospel to those that despise its ministers. The Alexandrian Origen widens the saying furthest: the country of the prophets was Judea, and everyone knows what honour the prophets had there — indeed the proverb's truth reaches even the pagan teachers of wisdom, despised and put to death by their own fellow-citizens. That such a proverb should have four rival referents — Nazareth, Capernaum, Galilee, Judea — is itself the story of the next verse.
44:
What country has no honour for its prophet? On this parenthesis the cloud fractures into four positions, honestly catalogued by the grammatical-historical Robertson: in the Synoptics the proverb points to Nazareth, where Jesus was twice rejected (Mark 6:4; Luke 4:24); but John can hardly mean Judea, "for he had already explained that Jesus was leaving Judea because he was too popular there" (John 4:1-3); and if he means Galilee, the very next verse records a cordial Galilean welcome. Robertson nevertheless settles on Galilee: John is explaining the motive of Jesus in going where he had not yet laboured and had no such fame as in Judea and Samaria. He adds two sober notes — a similar proverb circulated in Plutarch, Pliny, and Seneca, and John need not mean that Jesus spoke the words on this occasion at all; the Evangelist quotes a saying his readers knew.

Ellicott takes the knife to the geographical escape-routes altogether: solutions like "Judea," "Nazareth as distinct from Galilee," or "lower Galilee" are "brought to, not from, the text." His reading turns the proverb from an obstacle into the explanation: Jesus knew the principle that a prophet's own friends are the last to hear his message, and therefore he came to his own country only when the message had first been received in Judea and Samaria, and his countrymen had themselves seen his work at the Passover. "The honour is brought from without. It does not arise in his own country." Barnes offers both grammatical bridges — either "he went to Galilee, but not to Nazareth, for he testified..." or "he went although he had said it, because he foreknew that many Galileans would believe."

Calvin, whose treatment of the proverb the compilers preserved, sides with Cyril of Alexandria against both Augustine's subtlety (that Christ honoured Samaria above his own Galilee) and Chrysostom's Capernaum: the "country" is Nazareth, which the other three Evangelists name when they report the saying; despised in his native city, Christ withdrew to another part of Galilee. He then dissects the proverb itself with characteristic sobriety: it is a common saying, "a graceful expression of what commonly and most frequently happens," and proverbs must not be pressed into rigid universal accuracy — sometimes a prophet is honoured at home. Its truth has two roots: we despise all our lives those "whom we have heard crying in the cradle, and whom we have seen acting foolishly in their boyhood," and envy prevails most among acquaintances; though Calvin suspects the saying really arose from the shameful record of Israel itself, whose own prophets were nowhere less honoured than in their own country. If the general sense be taken, he notes, "prophet" simply means any teacher, as Paul calls the pagan Epimenides a prophet (Titus 1:12). Clarke and Wesley are content with the Synoptic key (Matthew 13:57): Jesus bore testimony to the general truth of the proverb, and avoided the one town where it applied to him with full force.
45:
A welcome, then — and every witness asks what it was worth. Robertson notes the irony in the grammar itself: Jesus "had evidently anticipated a quiet arrival," yet the Galileans welcomed him, and the reason stands in the perfect participle — having seen all that he did at the feast in Jerusalem, the unrecorded signs of John 2:23 that had made a stir at that first Passover. He adds the sociological point: the Samaritans had not gone up to the feast, so Jesus came to them a stranger; the Galileans, orthodox Jews, had gone, and so came home predisposed in his favour. Barnes reads the reception generously — they received him kindly, as a messenger of God, for they had seen his miracles and believed; Clarke says roundly that they received him "as the promised Messiah."

Calvin is cooler. Whether this honour lasted, he observes, we have no means of determining, "for there is nothing to which men are more prone than forgetfulness of the gifts of God." John's design in recording it is more modest than a revival: to show that Christ's Jerusalem miracles had been wrought before many witnesses, so that their report spread far and wide — and to display one real advantage of miracles, that "they prepare the way for doctrine, for they cause reverence to be paid to Christ." The Alexandrian Origen fills in what the Galileans had actually seen: the expulsion of the sheep-sellers and money-changers from the temple had impressed them with a strong idea of his majesty — a work showing his power no less than opening blind eyes — and probably other miracles besides; and he draws the quiet providence in the timing: because the Galileans were at the feast where they saw Jesus, they were prepared to receive him when he came; otherwise they would have rejected him, or he, knowing their unprepared state, would not have gone near them.

The Puritan Matthew Henry harvests the practical lessons. These Galileans lay far from Jerusalem, their road ran through unfriendly Samaria, yet in obedience to God's command they went up to the feast — and there they became acquainted with Christ: "they that are diligent and constant in attending on public ordinances some time or other meet with more spiritual benefit than they expect." And he notices for whom the Jerusalem miracles bore fruit: wrought for the benefit of those at Jerusalem, they profited most the Galileans who happened to be there — as the word preached to a mixed multitude may edify the occasional hearers more than the constant auditory. The Latin Bede, who also remarks that "they also went to the feast," cannot leave the homecoming unallegorized: the Lord's return to his own country signifies that at the end of the world, when the Gentiles have been confirmed in the faith by the two precepts of love, he will return to his country — that is, to Judea.
46:
Why Cana again? Ellicott hears the answer in the Evangelist's own aside — it was the place "where he made the water wine" (John 2:1-11), the scene of the first manifested glory; Robertson adds that the memory of that outstanding miracle would linger in the town and gave Jesus friends there. The Antiochene Chrysostom supplies the pastoral motive: having attended a marriage there before, he now goes to convert the people and to confirm by his presence the faith his miracle had produced; and the Latin Augustine remembers how much there was still to do — at the crowded wedding feast only his disciples had believed in consequence of that great miracle, so he "visits the city again, in order to try a second time to convert them."

Then the new figure enters, and the witnesses fall to work on one Greek word: what is a basilikos? Not "a little king," Calvin insists against Erasmus's Latin regulus, for Galilee then had no resident dukes or barons — rather some gentleman of Herod's court, though he grants plausibility to those who think him an envoy of Caesar. Ellicott agrees on philological grounds: the word, an adjective formed from "king" and found nowhere else in the New Testament, is frequent in Josephus for a courtier or civil or military officer, never for royalty; the king in question is almost certainly Herod Antipas, styled king in Matthew 14:9 and Mark 6:14. Clarke reports Jerome's rendering, an officer of the palace, and the old guesses — Chuza, Herod's steward (Luke 8:3), or Manaen (Acts 13:1) — "one of these opinions may be true, but all solid proof is wanting"; Ellicott finds the Chuza conjecture attractive, since his wife's presence among the ministering women would be beautifully explained by the restoration of her child. Lightfoot makes him an Herodian on principle, not a mere attendant (Matthew 22:16); the Alexandrian Origen notes it is nowhere said that he was a Jew.

Chrysostom heads off the harmonizers who would merge him with the centurion of Matthew 8:5: the centurion begged Christ not to come under his roof, this man labours to bring Christ to his house; the one met Jesus near Capernaum, the other comes out to Cana; the one pleaded for a paralysed servant, the other for a fevered son. Theophylact adds a sharp editorial point — the Evangelist recalls the Cana miracle precisely "to express the praise due to the Samaritans," for the Galileans' welcome leaned on wonders seen, while Sychar had believed a bare word. And Calvin closes the introductions with a note on design: the rank of the personage is expressly mentioned "because it made the miracle the more illustrious" — the sickness reaching a great man's house so that grace might reach a great man's household. Barnes fixes the geography for what follows: the father's residence was Capernaum, about a day's journey from Cana, where Jesus now was.
47:
News travels first in this verse. Gill notes that the fame of Christ, his doctrine and miracles, "was spread every where," so that his movements were known and talked of; Ellicott measures the reach — Capernaum lay twenty to twenty-five miles from Cana, and the report of the return to Galilee had covered that whole area; Robertson catches the pace in the tenses: the father, hearing, "went off at once," and then "began to beg and kept it up," for his son was at the point of death — the same idiom of impending death John will use of Jesus himself (John 11:51).

The grammatical-historical Barnes weighs the journey as an act of faith with a flaw in it. That a man high in office did not refuse to go personally to a Galilean teacher, travelling to meet him and beseeching him, showed strong faith and a father's heart — "the rich and the poor, the high and the low, must come personally as humble suppliants." But he erred in supposing that Jesus could heal only in person: the miracles hitherto had been wrought on those present, and one design of this miracle was to break that assumption. The Puritan Matthew Henry draws the same double portrait: sincerity in the faith — he believed Christ could cure a case the physicians had likely given over — and infirmity in it, for like Naaman he expected the healer to come and stand over the patient (2 Kings 5:11); how unlike the centurion, whose "I am not worthy that thou shouldest come under my roof" (Matthew 8:8) shames the nobleman's insistence. Henry's rule follows: "We are encouraged to pray, but we are not allowed to prescribe."

Calvin turns the diagnosis doctrinal. That he applies to Christ at all is some evidence of faith; but when he limits the manner of the help, "that shows how ignorant he was," for he views the power of Christ as inseparably attached to his bodily presence — he had formed no higher notion of Christ than of a prophet sent from God, with authority and power to prove his commission by miracles. This fault, Calvin observes, Christ overlooks for the moment; the severe answer of the next verse aims at a wider target. Wesley confines himself to a single word of geography that every pilgrim would recognize: "come down — for Cana stood much higher than Capernaum," perched in the hills above the lake basin. So the verse leaves the father mid-plea: his affection unimpeachable — he would spare no pains for a dying son — his courtesy real, his theology fifteen miles too small, still binding the Lord of heaven and earth to the sickroom he asks him to visit.
48:
The severity of this answer startles the commentators before anything else. Gill hears "some degree of roughness in his speech, and severity in his countenance"; Calvin asks the question outright: how comes it that Christ is now so harsh, "who is wont to receive kindly others who desire miracles"? His answer begins from the little word all the witnesses seize on — the ye is plural. Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, and Calvin agree that the rebuke passes over the nobleman's head to the Galileans at large, and beyond them to the nation: a people who had been educated in the word of God from infancy, yet had no reverence for doctrine unless aroused by wonders, and who, when the wonders came, remained in stupidity and amazement rather than faith. It is Paul's charge in another form — the Jews require a sign (1 Corinthians 1:22) — and Calvin hears its exact echo in his own day: "Let them first perform miracles, and then we will lend an ear to their doctrine"; though God overwhelmed such men with miracles, they would be no whit more attentive.

The foil, nearly every witness notes, is Samaria. Wesley compresses it to a clause — "although the Samaritans believed without them" — and Barnes and Clarke expand the contrast: the men of Sychar, with no miracle at all, believed on a word; the orthodox Galileans must be perpetually shown signs and wonders. Ellicott sharpens it into an epigram: faith that demands a miracle "was not faith, but sight." Clarke is careful, though, to keep the rebuke proportioned: our Lord does not say the man had no faith — else he had never ridden from Capernaum — but that he had not enough, since he could not trust Christ to heal at a distance; and Clarke adds the theological caution that the grace of working miracles is quite distinct from saving grace (1 Corinthians 13:2). Robertson reads the tone as disappointment more than anger: Jesus, who never discounts his own signs, is grieved to find himself regarded in Galilee as a mere miracle-worker — and the double negative is the strongest the language affords, "ye will in no wise believe."

The Latin Augustine presses the charge deepest into the soul: the request as first made was a test — as if the man's object were to try what Christ was and what he could do — and the Lord "would have the mind of the believer so raised above all mutable things, as not to seek even for miracles," for miracles, though sent from heaven, are wrought upon mutable matter. Lightfoot, from the rabbinic side, finds the rebuke aimed with special justice at Herod's court: they had heard the Baptist — the tetrarch himself with a kind of veneration — yet because John "did no miracle" (John 10:41) he was the more easily disbelieved and thrown into prison. Sign-hunger, on this reading, was the very disease of the nobleman's own circle, and Christ lances it before he heals the fever.
49:
He does not argue; he only pleads again. Most of the witnesses are struck by what the reproof did not do to this courtier. Calvin holds him up as a small miracle of grace in himself: courtiers are usually fastidious and haughty, unwilling to be treated with harshness, "but this man, humbled by his necessitous case, and by the dread of losing his son, does not burst into a passion or murmur when Christ speaks to him roughly, but passes by that reproof in modest silence" — and we, Calvin adds, are astonishingly delicate and fretful until adversity subdues our pride. The Puritan Matthew Henry likewise credits him: he took the reproof patiently and did not take it for a denial, but "continued to wrestle till he prevailed" — it is no privilege of peerage to be above the reproofs of Christ's word, and it is a sign of a good temper in great men when they can be told of their faults and not be angry.

Yet the plea itself still limps, and the cloud is unsparing about where. "Come down, ere my child die" assumes two falsehoods at once. Gregory the Great names the first: his faith was deficient in that he thought the Lord could not save unless personally present. Robertson, citing Bengel, names the second: he supposed Jesus had power only before death — precisely the assumption of Martha and Mary at Bethany (John 11:21; John 11:32). Henry works out the arithmetic of that error: he believes Christ can heal a sick child but not raise a dead one — forgetting that Elijah and Elisha raised dead children, "and is Christ's power inferior to theirs?" — and his "come down, ere my child die" hurries the Lord "as if there were danger of Christ's slipping his time," whereas he that believeth does not make haste. The Antiochene Chrysostom presses hardest: mark his earthly mind, hurrying Christ along; possibly he asked in sheer unbelief, for fathers in extremity consult even those they do not rely on, unwilling to leave any means untried — had he had strong reliance, he would have sought Christ in Judea long before. Clarke detects wounded dignity as well: the man seems to feel himself hurt that the Lord did not come at his first entreaty, and his spirit is "not much unlike that of Naaman the Syrian" (2 Kings 5:11).

Ellicott alone turns the criticism inside out into one of the tenderest sentences in the cloud: "human sorrow is the birth-pang of faith." The sense of utter powerlessness casts the soul on the Strong One for strength; the faith is still weak — it cannot yet conceive that Christ may speak the word and heal — but it is there, feeling that his presence could save, and it "pleads as a father for his son." Robertson hears the same tenderness in the Greek diminutive the father chooses: not "my son" now, but "my little child." Calvin concludes the balance judicially: since he perseveres in asking and at length obtains his wish, Christ's reproof was never meant to reject him, but to correct the fault that obstructed the entrance of true faith.
50:
Calvin calls what happens here "the astonishing kindness and condescension of Christ": he bears with the man's ignorance, and stretches his power beyond what had been asked. The father requested a house-call; Christ "grants much more than he had requested — for he testifies as to the present health of his son." So, Calvin observes, our Heavenly Father often declines the particulars of our wishes precisely in order to relieve us by unexpected methods, "that we may learn not to prescribe to him in anything." Barnes and Clarke draw out the same paradox: Jesus granted the main wish while refusing the manner — and the refusal was the mercy, for, as Clarke argues, had the Lord walked down to Capernaum, the man's unbelief could never have been fully cured; he would still have thought that power could not reach from Cana to Capernaum. By healing "apparently absent," Christ shows himself present by that energy through which he fills both the heavens and the earth. Gill relishes the pastoral tone of the dismissal, quoting the Persic version's paraphrase: "be not anxious, and go thy way."

The Latin Gregory turns the geography into doctrine: the Lord's answer implies that he is in a certain sense present where he is invited, even when absent from the place — he saves by his bare command, "even as by his will he created all things." And Gregory adds his famous observation on the two healings compared: the Redeemer would not go down to the nobleman's son, yet was ready to go to the centurion's servant — a deliberate blow to the pride that honours human wealth and greatness rather than the nature made in God's image; things made much of among men are to be despised, and things despised made much of. The Antiochene Chrysostom explains the difference by the faith found in each: in the centurion, confirmed faith, so the Lord offered to come; in the nobleman, faith still imperfect, so the Lord would not — and by refusing, enlightened him.

Then the hinge of the story: "the man believed the word that Jesus spake unto him, and went his way." Robertson savours both verbs — an aorist of instantaneous faith, an inchoative imperfect as he "started on his way," acting on what he believed. Ellicott marks the graduation: until now he had believed on the testimony of others; now he believes on account of the word of Christ himself. Calvin marvels at the economy of it: a man half a heathen profits more by hearing a single word than crowds profit by many sermons — a short promise "suddenly awoke new confidence in his mind, so that he believed the life of his son to be contained in a single word of Christ"; such promptitude is how the word of God ought always to be received. Only Clarke enters a small dissent about the completeness of the change, suspecting the faith still hung on a condition — "If I find on my return that my son is healed, I will believe" — and Chrysostom likewise grants that he believed, "not, however, wholly or completely"; the seventh-hour inquiry of v.52 will be their evidence.
51:
Ellicott walks the road home with him, and his imagination supplies what the terse narrative omits: many a long mile lay between Cana and the sick-bed, and many an anxious thought must have travelled with the father — "now faith would be strong, and now almost give way; but he travels on with the words, 'Thy son liveth,' which had come to him as a voice from heaven, sustaining and cheering him." Then, on the way down, the same words again — "Thy son liveth!" — but this time on the lips of his own servants, come up from Capernaum to meet him with the news he had carried in promise from Cana. The Alexandrian Origen notes in passing that the very fact of servants dispatched to meet their master displays his rank; the Antiochene Chrysostom reads their errand less sentimentally — they came to announce what had happened and so to prevent Christ from being brought down, "as He was no longer wanted," a household still thinking of the healer as a physician whose visit could now be cancelled.

Robertson attends to John's words for the boy, and finds a little ladder of tenderness in them: in v.49 the father had said "my little child"; in v.50 Jesus said "thy son"; here the servants say "thy boy" — the only place John uses that word — as the household's own familiar name for him. Gill supplies his chronological note: the father was "now going down" from Cana to Capernaum "the day after he had been with Christ," a detail that will matter when the servants say "yesterday."

Calvin, as usual, distils the scene into doctrine: here is described "the effect of faith, together with the efficacy of the word; for as Christ, by a word, restores to life this child who was just dying, so in one moment the father, by his faith, regains his son safe and sound." From that he builds a general comfort with an honest caveat. Whenever the Lord offers us his benefits, his power stands ready to accomplish all he promises — provided the door be not shut against him by our unbelief. Not that God always displays his arm instantly; delay is frequent, but never idle: "whenever he delays, he has always a good reason, and one that is highly advantageous to us," and often he is not delaying at all but contending with the obstacles we ourselves throw in the way. When his aid seems slow, Calvin counsels, let us examine how much concealed distrust there is in us; for God chooses to bestow his benefits on those who, "by opening the bosom of their faith, are ready to receive them," and no man's faith will finally prove fruitless — the promises of God, even when they seem to tarry, are in reality making great haste (Habakkuk 2:3).
52:
"Yesterday at the seventh hour the fever left him." The father's question that drew this answer divides the witnesses over his motive, and the answer itself divides them over the clock. On the motive, Gill insists he "did not at all hesitate about the truth of it," but asked the exact time in order to compare things together; Wesley makes the inquiry a model — "the more exactly the works of God are considered, the more faith is increased"; and Calvin sees more than curiosity in it: the courtier asked "by a secret impulse from God, that the truth of the miracle might be rendered more conspicuous," for our nature is wickedly disposed to extinguish the light of God's power, and Satan labours to hide his works; this circumstance leaves no room for the recovery to be ascribed to chance. Clarke, less charitably, thinks the father was still testing "whether his cure was the effect of some natural cause, or whether it was done by the power of Christ." Ellicott notices the tell-tale wording: he asks when the boy "began to amend," expecting the slow curve of an abating fever — while the servants answer in another register altogether: the fever left him, suddenly, wholly, as a power passed into a body at the point of death. Robertson supplies the Greek colour: the comparative adverb of the question means "took a turn, got better," and appears in the papyri and Arrian in a physician's cheerful "thou art doing finely."

Then the hour. Barnes states the traditional reckoning: the seventh hour from sunrise, about one o'clock in the afternoon. Robertson takes the other side: Roman time, seven in the evening; and Clarke reports Macknight's ingenious defence of that view — if the word was spoken at seven at night, a day's journey from home, that would itself explain why the Lord did not accompany the nobleman. Ellicott argues the Jewish reckoning at length: John gives no sufficient reason to think he counts hours in the western manner (John 1:39), and Galilean servants certainly would not; the real puzzle — how one o'clock could be "yesterday" if the father, who "clearly went at once," had only twenty-five miles to cover — dissolves once we remember that the Jewish day ended at sunset: healed at the seventh hour, the boy was already healed "yesterday" to servants speaking after nightfall, leaving an interval of only five or six hours between the word at Cana and its confirmation on the road.

The Puritan Matthew Henry, comparing the note-taking of father and servants, finds two props for faith in the precision itself: the cure was sudden, not gradual — they name the time "not about, but at the seventh hour," for with Christ it was dictum factum, he spoke and it was done, not set a-doing like physic; and it fell at the very hour of the word, for such "synchronisms and coincidents of events add very much to the beauty and harmony of Providence" — deliverance at the very time promised, as Israel's (Exodus 12:41), or at the very time prayed for, as Peter's (Acts 12:12).
53:
"Himself believed" — but had he not believed already, back at v.50? Calvin faces the apparent absurdity squarely: the Evangelist seems to record the commencement of faith in a man whose faith he has already commended. His solution is a portrait of faith's stages: as a Jew educated in the law, the man had some taste of faith before ever he came to Cana; at Cana he gained a particular faith, which extended no farther than his son's life; but now he believes in another manner altogether — embracing the doctrine of Christ, professing himself a disciple, acknowledging Christ to be the Son of God. The Latin Bede had drawn the same ladder centuries earlier: faith, like the other virtues, is formed gradually and has its beginning, its growth, and its maturity — begun when he asked for the recovery, grown when he believed the word "thy son liveth," matured at the servants' announcement; and Augustine adds the astringent corollary that if he only believed fully when the hours were compared, he did not truly believe when he first made his petition. Ellicott traces the same development and marks its place in John's design, who shows faith ripening by degrees in the disciples (John 2:11) and the Samaritans (John 4:41-42) alike — and he finds a second miracle folded inside the first: a new life passes into the father's own spirit, and he too, "bound in the death-grasp of a formal religion, liveth."

"And his whole house." Robertson calls this the first example in the Gospel record of a whole family believing, ancestor of Crispus and his household at Corinth (Acts 18:8). Barnes anatomizes how such conversions run: the sickness of one son issued in the faith of all — affliction is often the means of great good, a father may be the instrument of his children's salvation, and there is a peculiar charm when piety enters the houses of the great, where example and influence reach so far. Clarke observes that the family, "impressed with the great kindness of God in sending health to the child, were the more easily led to believe": they had doubtless thought God was dealing hardly with them, "but now they see that in very faithfulness God had afflicted them."

Clarke also brings forward a curiosity from the Talmud: the story in Beracoth of Rabbi Chanina praying on his roof for the sick son of Rabban Gamaliel, announcing "his fever has departed," and the disciples writing down the hour — which proved the very hour the fever left the boy. His verdict on the parallel is Schoettgen's: "one egg is not more like to another than this fable to the evangelical narration" — very possibly, he thinks, stolen from this holy source. Barnes closes with the plain apologetic force of the scene itself: father in anxious transit, servants eyewitnesses at home, the healer twenty-five miles away — "everything in the case bears the mark of being the simple energy of God," put forth with equal ease afar or near; and thus, he adds, can Christ save the sinner.
54:
Preaching on this closing verse, the Antiochene Chrysostom begins with a miner's maxim: as a man skilled in gold mines will not overlook the smallest vein, so in the holy Scriptures it is impossible without loss to pass by one jot or tittle, "for they all are uttered by the Holy Spirit, and nothing useless is written in them." Even the little word second, he insists, is added with a purpose — "to exalt yet more the praise of the Samaritans, by showing that even when a second miracle had been wrought," the Galileans who beheld it had not yet risen as high as those at Sychar who had seen none at all. The comparison that governs the whole chapter thus sounds once more in its last line.

What exactly does "again the second" count? Gill answers: the second wrought in that place, Cana of Galilee — for between the wedding and this healing Jesus had done many signs in Jerusalem and Judea (John 2:23; John 3:2). Clarke reports Bishop Pearce's solution: construe "again" with "he was come," as in v.46 — this was the second sign done after coming out of Judea into Galilee, which removes the confusion of the common version. Ellicott reaches the same sense by strict translation, since the Greek has no article: "This again, a second sign, did Jesus" — and, since in John every sign carries its deeper teaching, he reads the lesson of this one: at Cana's first sign individual disciples believed and followed; now, for the first time, "the family is the unit in the Christian life," and the father, himself taught to read the sign, becomes the first teacher and representative of the first Christian household.

Ellicott also settles accounts with a suspicion as old as Irenaeus — that this narrative and the centurion's servant (Matthew 8:5; Luke 7:2) are one story twice told. He tables the differences: a nobleman here, a centurion there; a son, not a servant; pleading in person, not through elders; a Jew, not a Gentile; the word spoken at Cana, not Capernaum; a fever, not paralysis; a father begging Christ to come down, a centurion deprecating his coming; blame for half-faith here, marvelling at great faith there — in their totality "little short of proof" that John has added a sign the earlier Gospels do not record; repetition, after all, is part of the method of every great teacher. Clarke closes the chapter as Gill's Puritan forebears would have wished, reading back over the whole of it the doctrine of particular providence: the persecution that drove Jesus from Judea, the road that had to pass through Samaria, the weariness that sat him on the well, the errand that removed the disciples, the water-pot that brought the woman — every common occurrence pressed into the accomplishment of the designs of God's mercy and love; there is not a circumstance in our lives, he concludes, that God will not make subservient to our salvation, "if we have a simple heart and a teachable spirit."
